“Yes, my child. Help without asking too many questions and surely you will be rewarded.”

References

  • Byrne, D (2024). Is it time to incorporate AI skills into our writing lessons? Modern English Teacher, Volume 33 | Issue4 | July/August 2024, Pavilion Publications. www.modernenglishteacher.com
  • Maley, A. (2015) Interview with Alan Maley on Teaching and Learning Creative Writing. By Ruzbeh Babaee published in the International Journal of Comparative Literature & Translation Studies ISSN 2202-9451 Vol. 3 No. 3; July. Australian International Academic Centre, Australia 2015. http://dx.doi.org/10.7575/aiac.ijclts.v.3n.3p.77

The author is an Assistant Professor at SDNB Vaishnav College for women, Chennai. She is a passionate, reflective teacher and lifelong learner. She can be reached at nageethirumalai@yahoo.co.in.

item.title: 'Curiosity' by NCERT: transforming science education with competency-based learning
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Shubhangi Raajput

NCERT's new class 6 science textbook "Curiosity" marks a significant shift in India's educational landscape, aligning with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and National Curriculum Framework for School Education (NCF-SE) 2023. This innovative textbook represents a departure from traditional rote learning methods, embracing a more holistic approach that combines competency-based learning with India's rich scientific heritage. Following are the key features of the new textbook.

In this article, we will explore two major aspects of the textbook, i.e., competency-based learning and the integration of the Indian Knowledge System (IKS).

Shaping education through competencies
Competency-based learning (CBL), the textbook's main emphasis, is demonstrated by thoughtfully crafted elements that emphasize skill development rather than memorization. Through interactive exercises, real-world applications, and practical experiments, students get exposed to scientific ideas. For example, students conduct hands-on experiments to investigate how plants adapt to various settings rather than merely learning the definition of photosynthesis.
Higher-order thinking skills are the focus of a newly redesigned evaluation technique. Case studies and real-world issues that call for analytical and critical thinking have supplanted traditional question-answer forms. Students studying the water cycle, for instance, might address actual water scarcity problems by using their knowledge of evaporation and condensation to suggest solutions.

Connecting contemporary science and traditional wisdom
"Curiosity" stands out for its smooth blending of modern scientific concepts with IKS. The textbook uses some instances to highlight India's great scientific legacy:
A real example of metallurgy that showcases advanced ancient techniques of engineering is the Iron Pillar of Delhi. Step-wells (baolis) and check dams (Johads), two traditional water-conservation techniques, are shown with contemporary sustainability concepts, highlighting the value of indigenous wisdom in tackling today's environmental issues.
Along with including traditional medicine and Ayurvedic concepts, the textbook explains the scientific rationale for the usage of popular plants like turmeric and neem. Students gain an appreciation for how conventional methods fit with current scientific knowledge thanks to this method.

Developing 21st century competencies
By encouraging debates and group projects, "Curiosity" helps students learn how to work together and communicate effectively. By integrating physics, mathematics, social sciences, and environmental studies, the interdisciplinary method offers a more thorough comprehension of scientific ideas.
Students are encouraged to assess their comprehension and think about the real-world applicability of scientific ideas in their daily lives through regular "Think and Reflect" portions. This part of self-assessment aids in the development of metacognitive abilities that are essential for lifelong learning.

Curiosity represents a paradigm shift in science education, making it more learner-centric, application-based, and competency-driven. By fostering critical thinking, problem-solving, collaboration, and real-world application, it ensures that students develop not just knowledge, but the skills necessary to thrive in an ever-changing world. It integrates India’s rich scientific heritage with modern scientific understanding, helping students appreciate the depth of India’s contributions to global science. By embedding IKS in various chapters, it ensures that learning is not just global and contemporary but also deeply rooted in indigenous knowledge.

Characteristics of chapter end assessment:

Keeping in mind the spirit of NEP 2020 and NCF, the assessment encompasses various elements

  • The assessment effectively integrates knowledge, analysis, application, and inquiry-based learning.
  • It caters to different cognitive levels, from recall to problem-solving.
  • Engages students in real-world problem-solving and awareness-building regarding nutrition and food sustainability.

Emphasis on contextualization

Post every exercise there are various suggestive extended learning activities mentioned for contextualization. These activities can be effectively used by teachers to connect the dots from various subjects and create a sense of national pride.

Inclusion of Indian scientists’ contribution

This new textbook has made a conscious effort to include the contribution of Indian scientists under the caption “Know your scientist”

Creation of future citizens

There are a lot of activities in the text as well as extended learning, which are designed carefully. These can easily be emulated in the classroom in various ways. The intent is to create awareness among young learners about local and global issues. The fun yet organized activities are excellent ways to create more informed and sensitive future citizens.

In conclusion, this textbook represents a progressive step towards modernizing science education in India. Its alignment with NEP 2020 and NCF-SE 2023 ensures that students receive a holistic, culturally enriched, and competency-based education, preparing them to navigate and contribute effectively to the contemporary world.

Shubangi Rajput is Curriculum Development Manager, Aditya Birla Education Academy, Gujarat Project. She can be reached at raajputshubhangi@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shubhangi Raajput
Indian textbooks are changing and for the better. Here's an educator's experience with the new NCERT class 6 textbook for science.

item.title: A heartfelt goodbye
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Fathima Binsiya M

I had planned to go home a week before the Pongal vacation. The day before my departure was hectic, both at school and at home. Since I often found it difficult to engage with my students' little moments during class hours, I often visited them during snack breaks. Today, I visited not just to see them, but to say bye to my dearest students.

As I walked through the corridor toward my class, I reflected on how being a class teacher is a great responsibility. At home, I have only my daughter to take care of, but at school, I have 21 students—each with their own stories, joys, and concerns. I have to nurture them, guide them, solve their problems, scold them when necessary, and learn from them.

It was the evening snack break when I entered the classroom. The moment they saw me, the kids surrounded me—some with complaints, some for a fun chat, and some to share their snacks. I took a small bite from each snack box they offered.

Then, I noticed a snack box with a note on top: "Share with your friends." Seeing this, I felt a sudden excitement and asked, "Whose box is this?"

Aneesh stepped forward and said, "It’s mine." "Who wrote this?" I asked.

"My mother sent this during lunch time."

In that chaotic yet heartwarming moment, this small gesture felt special. Currently, I’m teaching them about food waste and ways to reduce it. Sharing with others is one of the methods we had discussed. I truly believe that rather than repeating lessons a hundred times, these small, experiences shape young minds. They teach the values of love, care, and compassion in ways that words alone cannot. Some lessons in life we may memorize, but their true meaning only becomes clear when we live them—and that is when real change happens.

Fathima Binsiya M is an educator from Malappuram, Kerala. Currently, she is working as a teacher of Global Perspectives at Aimee International School in Vijayawada, Andhra Pradesh. She can be reached at mfbinsiya1@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Fathima Binsiya M
Teachers and students, especially at the primary level, form a beautiful bond that gives rise to many sweet moments. A teacher shares one such sweet incident.

item.title: My little food champions
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Madhulika Singh

Young kids carry snack and tiffin boxes to school. It is important that we teach them to finish the food in their boxes, so that they don’t waste it.

Why we should not waste food

  1.  Farmers work hard to grow our food. Wasting it is unfair to their efforts.
  2. Finishing food shows good discipline and manners.
  3. Eating all the food in the tiffin box ensures that children get the nutrition they need to grow.
  4. Less food waste means fewer resources, such as water and energy, are wasted.

How can children save food?

  • Parents should pack just enough food that children can eat easily
  • Kids should focus on eating instead of talking or playing during lunchtime.
  • If some food is left, they should close the tiffin box properly and take it back home instead of throwing it away.
  • If they have extra food, they can share it with a friend (if the school allows it).
  • If kids don’t like certain food items, parents can pack something healthier that they will finish.

To stop my students wasting food, I started keeping a tray on my table and as soon as recess would get over, I would ask my students to put the leftover portion of their food in the tray. I would count the number of portions kept in the tray daily and maintain a record, which I would share with the students. Gradually, the students realized realized what they were doing, and today each and every child is cautious about wastage.

Not wasting food brought in the tiffin box is a small step that makes a big difference. It teaches children responsibility, good eating habits, and respect for food. By following these simple steps, kids in classes 1 and 2 can become food-saving champions!

 Madhulika Singh is Academic Head from Sunbeam School Bhagwanpur, Varanasi. She has been working in this institution for the last 25 years. She can be reached at madhulikasingh1972@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Madhulika Singh
Food wastage is a real concern and teaching children this from an early age is very important. Here's how this teacher taught her young students the good habit of not wasting food.

item.title: The importance of savings and financial literacy from an early age
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Pratima Kishore Pai

I strongly believe in the saying "wealth builds wealth." With the right guidance, you can grow your wealth. Today’s youth live fast-paced lives. They want everything—expensive cars, houses, and all the best branded items at a young age. There’s nothing wrong with wanting to live well, but in their rush to own everything, many fall into debt.

Is buying a house really a good idea? Many would say yes, but if you think about it, property only increases in value significantly if it's an independent house. A flat might not appreciate as much over time. With the right knowledge, young people can better understand investments and how they work.

Social media often bring up reels which suggest that you can get rich quickly by making small investments. But in most cases, this is misleading, and it could be a trap to con people of their hard-earned money.

CBSE has taken a great step by introducing skill education for students from class 6. Among the skill subjects, financial literacy is offered, giving students the chance to learn about investments and stock trading. They even get to practice mock trading, which helps students understand how it all works.

If students learn about finances at an early age, they will be more careful when making investment decisions in the future. They’ll weigh the pros and cons and make smarter choices. Financial literacy helps them understand the power of compounding, allowing them to save more money.

Students who are financially literate from a young age develop responsible financial habits that stay with them throughout their lives. They will learn how to earn, spend, save, and invest wisely.

Financially literate people are also better at avoiding credit card debt, student loans, and other high-interest debt. Understanding borrowing and how to manage it will enable them to help and avoid the hassle of unnecessary debt.

Learning about basic investments like stocks, bonds, and mutual funds at an early age can lead to better opportunities for building wealth in the future, including investments in real estate and retirement savings. This will ensure that you can retire early if you want to and go globetrotting whenever you want to.

Managing finances early on gives people the confidence and skills to achieve financial independence. They will always walk that extra mile to ensure financial stability and possibly have no regrets. This means they can make their own decisions about money and won’t have to depend on others for support in adulthood.

Everyone faces financial ups and downs. Being financially literate helps people handle tough situations they may come across in life like losing a job or dealing with unexpected expenses. Knowing how to budget, save, and seek advice makes these challenges easier to handle.

Teaching kids good financial habits can have a lasting effect, not just on them but also on future generations. If children learn about saving, budgeting, and investing early, they can break cycles of financial struggle and create a culture of financial security. We can involve our kids in creating a monthly budget for household expenses so they understand that when money is limited, they should prioritize their needs based on necessity. Encouraging children to read newspapers like the Economic Times or financial magazines will help develop their knowledge and understanding of companies that attract investments, as well as how stocks rise and fall.

We should prioritize teaching our kids about finance. Opening a bank account for them allows them to store money and learn about interest rates and how the duration of an investment impacts its growth. They will also understand the importance of staying invested for the long term. It’s important to have discussions with your kids about banks and financial institutions, helping them learn about concepts like collateral and how loans work when there is no collateral. Such children may even research loan options available for entrepreneurs and the special benefits, particularly for female entrepreneurs. If more students pursue entrepreneurship, they will hire people as their businesses grow, which could help reduce poverty and unemployment in our country.

Understanding finances from a young age helps people set realistic goals, like saving for something big, investing for the future, or planning for retirement. People who are financially smart are more likely to reach their long-term goals because they manage their money carefully.

Pratima Kishore Pai is passionate about teaching and finds great joy in being around children, as their curiosity and enthusiasm inspires her to explore new ideas. She enjoys delving into various forms of art and embracing creative pursuits. She also has a keen interest in academic content development. Her siblings are her biggest cheerleaders, offering constant support in all her endeavours.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pratima Kishore Pai
Managing finances is a very important life skill and the sooner we introduce children to financial literacy, the better they will be at managing their finances.

item.title: Schools in India today
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

Today, the economic disparity in our society is abysmally high and this disparity is only increasing. Not only does the top 1% of India’s population own more than 40% of its wealth but this 1% has also seen its wealth rise by more than 50% during recent decades. All of this has been written enough to sound cliched but unfortunately this is the story of our times.

So, how are the schools placed amidst all this?

One way to look at the schools is the boards they are affiliated to. Broadly, we have the IB, IGCSE (Cambridge) and ICSE schools at the top, read - the most expensive schools. Then, somewhere in the middle are the CBSE schools. And, at the bottom of the pyramid lie the state board schools, including government run schools. The scenario becomes stark when we talk numbers. The total number of IB, IGCSE (Cambridge) and ICSE schools, all put together, is less than 5,000; while the total number of schools in India is around 15,00,000.

Fees the schools charge or the goodwill they harbour are not necessarily driven by the boards. However, schools the children attend are not just influenced but are largely determined by their parents’ wealth. And, these numbers mirror the inequalities in our society.  

These schools for the elite are miles ahead of the schools where the economically disadvantaged study, be it the infrastructure i.e. the classrooms, subject laboratories, internet paraphernalia, and sports facilities or otherwise i.e., the range of subjects offered, number of qualified teachers and opportunities for exposure.

The differences, though, lie deeper.

For the elite, the small minority,  schools are increasingly like any other product or service they purchase. Not very different, for example, from the restaurants they frequent - where they ‘order’ and ‘pay’ for what they desire, ‘consume’ what they then feel like, and nonchalantly ‘dispose’ the rest. These schools too increasingly treat parents as ‘clients’ and many a time accept the parents’ unreasonable demands to retain them. Not surprising then that they often end up like service providers who over-sell and underperform. On top of this, for many of these parents, the schools their children go to is a matter of pride, a bragging right at the parties they attend.

For the economically disadvantaged, the clear majority, the scenario is different - they retain some modicum of respect for the schools. They look up to the schools, as the schools hold the promise of a better future for them. And children in these schools still have a spark in their eyes - a hunger for learning. Though many of them also attend schools to escape from their day-to-day lives - lives that are not exactly pleasant. Teachers, in these schools, have an upper hand and it is not uncommon to see them misuse it while the parents believe that schools will miraculously help them rise up the socio-economic ladder.

Today, not only do the children from different economic classes attend different schools but schools have different meanings for different economic classes. This is yet another step that will make one part of the society oblivious to the existence of the other, yet another reminder that we as a society are going down a slippery slope.

Nimesh follows two religions - travel and food; he blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/ and can be reached at  nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
Indian schools function within the same widening economic divide that shapes the rest of our society. In a sense, they perpetuate the wealth gap in our society as elite institutions offer world-class infrastructure and opportunities, and millions of students attend schools with far fewer resources. This piece explores how wealth determines not just access to schools, but also what schools mean to different communities.

item.title: Creating school libraries as safe spaces
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Akhila Arora

Schools play a very crucial role when it comes to shaping a child’s development. This development is not just academic, but also social and emotional. Ever since the concept of holistic wellbeing has started gaining recognition, it has become more and more important to develop school libraries as safe spaces. This article sheds light on the pivotal role of creating supportive as well as inclusive environments within schools, particularly libraries as a space for fostering open dialogue, acceptance, and understanding of mental health issues.

The concept of safe spaces

Safe spaces are defined as environments where individuals feel secure, valued, and respected, irrespective of their backgrounds or identities. The school library, traditionally a hub of knowledge, is evolving into a space that not only nurtures intellectual growth but also provides a safe haven for students dealing with the complexities of mental health.At the heart of any effective school library programme lies the importance of individual understanding of each child. In recent years, due to increasing usage of technology and it being introduced much earlier in a child’s life, the boundaries between real and virtual lives have blurred. This causes a lot of mental stress and has resulted in a rise in children dealing with mental health issues. This alone offers reason enough to think about how our school libraries are meeting the need for a comfortable, welcoming, personalized learning environment by providing a non-judgmental and distraction-free atmosphere where students can be their true selves without having to prove themselves to anyone.

Breaking the stigma

One of the primary challenges in addressing mental health issues is the pervasive stigma that surrounds them. By transforming school libraries into safe spaces, educators and administrators can actively contribute to destigmatizing mental health. The library becomes a platform for resources that promote understanding, empathy, and awareness, and fostering an atmosphere where students feel comfortable seeking help and discussing their struggles.

Inclusive literature

A key element in the transformation of school libraries into safe spaces is curating literature that reflects diverse perspectives on mental health. It has been an established fact that representation matters a lot and hence inclusive literature that includes narratives depicting characters dealing with various mental health challenges is vital for providing students with relatable stories and role models. By incorporating such material into the library collection, schools send a powerful message of acceptance and acknowledgment of mental health as an integral aspect of the human experience.

Professional guidance

Librarians, as information facilitators, play the central role when it comes to shaping the culture of the school library. Moreover, librarians can serve as allies in creating an atmosphere of trust, ensuring that students feel comfortable in approaching them for support.

Open dialogue and support groups

In addition to housing relevant literature, school libraries can host open dialogue sessions and support groups focused on mental health. These forums provide students with the opportunity to share their experiences, learn from each other, and engage in conversations that break down the barriers surrounding mental health all the while mentioning representative literature. Librarians and school counsellors can collaborate to facilitate these sessions, fostering a sense of community within the school.

Conclusion

Creating school libraries as safe spaces is an imperative step in creating inclusive and supportive environments within educational institutions. By actively addressing mental health through literature, professional guidance, open dialogue, educational programs, and innovative technologies, schools can contribute greatly to fighting misconceptions and breaking the stigma surrounding mental health issues. The transformation of libraries into hubs of mental health and awareness regarding its issues reflects a commitment to the overall wellbeing of students, giving birth to a culture of acceptance, understanding, and empathy that extends beyond the classroom. In doing so, schools become catalysts for positive change, shaping the future of education in a manner that prioritizes the holistic development of every student.

Some “feel good” books:

  1. "The Happiness Project" by Gretchen Rubin
    • Gretchen Rubin takes readers on a year-long journey to discover what makes us happy and provides actionable advice on how to incorporate positive habits into our lives.
  2. "Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy" by David D. Burns
    • Dr. Burns, a renowned psychiatrist, provides practical tools and techniques from cognitive-behavioural therapy to help individuals overcome depression and improve their mental wellbeing.
  3. "Mindset: The New Psychology of Success" by Carol S. Dweck
    • Carol Dweck explores the concept of mindset and how one's beliefs about their abilities can impact success, happiness, and overall wellbeing.
  4. "Man's Search for Meaning" by Viktor E. Frankl
    • Based on his experiences as a Holocaust survivor, Viktor Frankl explores the pursuit of meaning in life, emphasizing the importance of finding purpose in the face of adversity.
  5. "Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones" by James Clear
    • James Clear provides a practical guide to understanding habits, making positive changes, and building a foundation for long-term wellbeing.
  6. "The Gifts of Imperfection: Let Go of Who You Think You're Supposed to Be and Embrace Who You Are" by Brené Brown
    • Brené Brown explores the power of vulnerability, shame, resilience, and the importance of embracing imperfections for a more wholehearted life.

Akhila Arora is the Librarian at Bal Bharati Public School, Rohini. She can be reached at akhila.arora@rh.balbharati.org
item.excerpt:encoded:
Akhila Arora
Libraries are not just reading spaces anymore, they can and should be a lot more.

item.title: An autobiography of a novice teacher
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Abdul Basith

My schooling                                                    

As students, the knowledge we assimilate is shaped by our experiences and the teachers who nurture us. A teacher plays a pivotal role, much like a gardener tending to a garden, ensuring that each child blossoms with love, patience, and guidance. Reflecting on my journey, I feel grateful to all those educators who shared their knowledge with me and inspired me to become a teacher myself and help struggling students like me.

As a student, I went to different schools in Kerala. Malayalam is my mother tongue, and all other languages were new for me. From primary school to the seventh grade, I studied in a Malayalam-medium school. However, in eighth grade, I transitioned to an English-medium school. I struggled with English grammar and spellings. But, the unwavering support of my parents and the encouragement of a dedicated teacher made this transition smoother. My English teacher’s influence instilled in me a love for learning and a commitment to teaching.

Stepping in to the education field

My decision to pursue education as a career was shaped by an individual who left a profound impact on my life. After completing my BA in English Literature, I contemplated enrolling in a B.Ed. program. However, the fieldwork aspect of an MA in Education piqued my interest, as it would provide me with firsthand experience of real classroom dynamics. The teaching internship I did during this course helped me understand that teaching extends beyond textbooks—it involves navigating ground realities and encouraging meaningful learning experiences.

The joy in those students’ eyes who were formerly pushed aside for falling behind is what inspires me the most as a teacher. The smiles we bring on and the hope we inspire are the sparks that keep me on walking this path. I witnessed an incident where a teacher hesitated to acknowledge a student’s effort because of her preconceived notions. However, the class’s collective insistence ensured that the student was rewarded fairly, reinforcing my belief that judgments and prejudices have no place in a teacher’s heart. Every child deserves a fair chance.

My journey as a teacher

I strive to create an inclusive and supportive environment where students feel valued and are encouraged to make mistakes and learn from them. In my classroom, boys and girls sit together in pairs. I frequently rearrange seating for group activities, ensuring that every student receives attention and engagement. My students see me as a friend who values their contributions, irrespective of their skill levels.

In my third week of teaching, students asked, "Will you remember us when you leave? Will you invite us to your wedding?" These questions reflected the bond I had built with them. Their excitement and curiosity during interactive activities affirmed their preference for engaging lessons over rote learning. I strive to balance my subject expertise with an understanding of students' perspectives, learning levels, and cultural backgrounds.

Reflecting on my teaching journey, I have realized that empathy, resilience, and faith in every child's potential are crucial. My first teaching experience was filled with both frustration and inspiration. I recall the heartbreak of hearing a third-grade student express his fear of English due to the corporal punishment he had received in the past. This moment solidified my commitment to providing a safe, nurturing learning environment.

One particular moment remains etched in my memory. I once wrote a Hindi word incorrectly on the board, and my students corrected me. This instance reinforced my belief that teaching is a reciprocal process of learning and growth. Small victories, such as a previously struggling student confidently narrating a story, made my efforts worthwhile. The spontaneous applause from his peers validated the inclusive environment I aimed to create.

I believe that all children can learn. This principle guides my lesson planning, ensuring inclusivity by accommodating diverse learning styles and abilities. Recognizing each child's unique strengths helps me encourage them to reach their full potential.

Building relationships with students has been one of the most fulfilling aspects of my teaching journey. Their enthusiastic greetings, engagement in lessons, and eagerness to share their thoughts highlight the relational foundation of teaching. On Children’s Day, their excitement to wear colourful clothes and participate in activities demonstrated the power of simple acknowledgments in creating a positive school culture. The depth of our bond was most evident on the last day of my teaching internship when students presented heartfelt gifts—pens, flowers, and handwritten notes. A girl tightly held my hand, her tear-filled eyes whispering, "Please don’t forget us." Her words affirmed the impact I had made, making every challenge and effort worthwhile.

Inspired by Palmer’s philosophy of "teaching beyond technique" and recognizing "the teacher within," (which he talks about in The Courage to Teach: Exploring the Inner Landscape of a Teacher’s Life) I realized that effective teaching is not solely about methods but about forming genuine connections with students. Teaching demands emotional investment and self-reflection, and the bonds I built with my students proved that education is fundamentally about relationships.

Starting my teaching career from scratch presented numerous challenges. The transition from being a student to standing in front of a classroom offered new insights into the realities of teaching. One striking experience was seeing a usually engaged student suddenly fall silent, her eyes brimming with tears. Upon inquiry, she shared that a lesson had triggered memories of her late father. At that moment, I realized that teachers must navigate not only academic learning but also students’ emotions and histories with empathy and care.

My journey forced me to confront and challenge my own biases. In the beginning, I observed that boys and girls hesitated to sit together in group activities. Initially, I viewed this as resistance, but deeper reflection revealed that cultural norms shaped this behaviour. Instead of imposing change, I gradually encouraged collaboration. This experience underscored the significance of cultural sensitivity in education.

As I reflect on my teaching journey, I realize that my smile has been my constant companion. My smile of warmth each morning while being greeted by my

students, my smile of strength in the class when my students understand what I am teaching, my smile of assurance when that student who was ignored by everyone raises his hand for answering a question, my smile of acceptance in difficult situations, and my smile of positivity when I see my students effectively using the TLMs (Teaching Learning Materials) I made. Just as my students' smiles lit up the classroom, mine lit up my heart. I have learned that teaching is not just about giving knowledge, but about sharing joy, empathy, and resilience. As I close this chapter, I smile with the hope that the lessons I have taught and the love I have shared will continue to shape the lives of my students.

Abdul Basith is a BA English Literature Graduate, now pursuing MA Education in Azim Premji University, Bangalore. He can be reached at abdul.basithp23_mae@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Abdul Basith
A young student-teacher shares the lessons he learnt while working with students during a short teaching internship stint that he did as part of his MA Education course.

item.title: Enhancing student engagement through technology-rich learning environments
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Kirti Sharma

EdTech implementation has transformed pedagogy and enabled educational institutions to embrace a new era of accessibility, innovation, and critical thinking. Therefore, forward-thinking schools are increasingly embracing digital technologies to prepare the next generation for a digital future. A K12 Digital Transformation-2023 Survey by Holon IQ shows that 76% of schools prioritize technology integration into education. This realization is metamorphosizing classrooms into innovation hubs.


EdTech revolution in India

EdTech is fundamental in bridging educational gaps, specifically in India's rural and underserved areas. Furthermore, GoI's New Education Policy 2020 supports this drive by improving the educational system's quality, integrity, and transparency. This initiative also complements India's education sector's Sustainable Development Goal 4, prioritizing inclusive and top-notch education for all.

India's EdTech sector will likely reach $29 billion by 2030 and grow at a CAGR of 25.8%. This indicates the growing influence of digital tools in education. EdTech tools will gain more traction in the future, driven by low-cost digital tools and public-private collaborations; nevertheless, this growth must be accompanied by robust regulations on ethical problems like privacy and equity so that all learners get the most from technology.

The role of personalization

Personalized learning systems and artificial intelligence (AI) are two of the most ground-breaking EdTech breakthroughs. AI customizes educational experiences to suit individual student needs and encourages a growth mindset among them. It also offers personalized lessons based on the real-time analysis of students' overall performances.

Also, virtual and augmented reality have turned classrooms into experiential learning centers. For instance, history students can now virtually visit an ancient civilization while biology students can perform virtual anatomy dissection. These EdTech tools make education engaging and impactful by bringing subjects to life. Recent industry reports claim that 50% of Indian schools will adopt virtual and augmented reality in three years.

EdTech tools also make students job-ready by instilling the traits of critical thinking, collaboration, and adaptability in them through interactive and collaborative platforms. For instance, group projects, enabled by cloud-based tools, enhance teamwork besides preparing students for the collaborative nature of modern workplaces. Additionally, EdTech emphasizes STEM education, soft skills development, and career-oriented programs to make students job-ready.

According to the World Economic Forum, 150 million new technology jobs will be created globally over the next few years. Additionally, by 2030, 77% of jobs will require digital skills. However, businesses are already facing a global skills shortage, with only 33% of tech roles currently filled by qualified workers. Thus, students must receive digital education to equip themselves with the technical skills needed to thrive in the future job market.


EdTech as social catalyst

EdTech tools have the potential to transform students more than academics do. It makes them digitally literate in an increasingly technology-dependent world. Modern classrooms are also emerging as safe spaces in which children learn about online etiquette, privacy, and the consequences of their digital footprints. EdTech also helps build international connections. Virtual classrooms allow students to work on projects with fellow students from other countries. Such collaborations open their minds and promote intercultural learning, preparing them for a global workforce that requires cross-cultural communication.


Changing role of teachers

EdTech is also helping teachers perform better. It helps them create meaningful learning experiences by leveraging their pedagogical strengths, customizing instruction, and engaging learners. A 2022 survey by the India EdTech Consortium shows that 82% of teachers believe technology has enhanced their teaching skills. With digital tools, teachers can become mentors to help their students develop critical skills. Additionally, professional development programs emphasizing technology integration make teachers into change agents.

Challenges and the way forward

Schools willing to adopt EdTech tools must invest in infrastructure, teacher training, and digital resources while considering data security issues. Bridging the rural-urban digital divide is another priority, especially in less connected and resource-constrained areas. Ethical concerns about data privacy, bias in algorithms, and equitable access also require strict regulatory frameworks. With AI-driven tools gathering vast amounts of data to personalize learning, strong policies are necessary to ensure this data is used responsibly.

Public-private partnerships and targeted investments in digital infrastructure can overcome these challenges. Educators and policymakers must also collaborate to build an environment where technology can enhance learning without compromising privacy.

Education is evolving to become more accessible and inclusive, driven by technology integration. Advanced EdTech tools enable institutions to provide adaptive and collaborative learning experiences, ensuring broader access for diverse learners. 

Kirti Sharma is Co-Ceo, Crimson schools.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kirti Sharma
Technology in education is here to stay. So instead of denying it or finding flaws, let us embrace it.

item.title: Making economics accessible
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

What is this book about?

“This book tells the stories of Nilou and her friends, a group of children and teens, and the adults who live with them. These children live in a village, one which could be in India or Kenya or Vietnam or elsewhere, and we see them deal with some ordinary and not-so-ordinary challenges.” Stories from “a world both very different and yet intensely familiar”.

These stories are connected by a set of common characters, and of course, the setting. Education, health, urban migration, elections, fear, beliefs, food, greenery, livelihood, government, and gender are the topics they touch upon. The issues they highlight are universal.

This is also a book written during or just after the COVID pandemic.

What works in the book?

Illustrations. This book is not text heavy. Its illustrations and fonts are different from those one comes across in Indian children’s books these days. The book size and production quality are friendly; they make flipping pages easy. Together, they keep the reader glued to the stories. My favourite page is the one on education (Nilou – Skips school) where children read “Ar-chi-mede The-o-rem” as “Archie made tea and rum”. It would be fun to discuss the illustrations with the children as also to get them to create fresh stories with these characters.

Text. The format of the book - personal narrative juxtaposed with fictional stories – works well. The personal narrative is peppered with lines that one can reread, “Something incredible happens when people come together and draw a map: they are able to remember everything they have in their village, and also when it was built, when it was repaired or improved, whether it is still in use, etc.”

Questions. The author hopes that the stories encourage readers to “ask many new questions”. The questions, especially those in the story on urban migration (Neso and Najy - Not afraid of the big city), make one wonder!

What does not work?

Approach. These stories present a world that is very simple and nice -- government servants are all helpful and people are taking up positive and good actions like “recycling” and “making nice things for all”. A world that is black and white. A world more from the times of Amar Chitra Katha of a couple of decades ago than the Eklavya and Ektara books of today. The book does not respect the intelligence of children.

Cliché. In the story on health (Afia is feeling sick) the pharmacist has “fake pills” and the villagers “love antibiotics”. The rich person is fat and a villain while “scientist” and “best way” go together. This book ends up underscoring the cliché. They reminded me of Stephen Fry’s well-known take on cliché, “It is a cliché that most clichés are true, but then like most clichés, that cliché is untrue.’

Authors’ lens. Across the stories, the solutions always come from the city – be it the nurse who got free bednets (health), or the government officer who makes payments for trees (greenery), or the supermarket (vegetables) or even the “ideas for activities” and microbanker (livelihood). And, for some reason, the villagers are always being challenged! Lines like this too don’t exactly help, “Have you ever wondered why some people continue to live in faraway villages when life becomes very difficult there?” Is life easier in the cities – especially for those who do not have surplus? In today’s times when increasingly the urban – rural divide is getting blurred what are “faraway villages”? And, “faraway” from whom?

Lack of clarity. Neither the title of the book nor the age group the book caters to are clear. On the one hand, the book talks of too many issues, on the other, it has little to offer that is fresh. The price of the book will make it out of bounds for those it talks about. And, could the title have been more apt?

Nimesh Ved loves walking, cycling, and is often seen amidst books. He enjoys putting his thoughts in words. He blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/. He can be reached at nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
Economics as a subject is introduced to children in middle school, but it is possible for children to engage with the subject even at younger ages through books like Poor Economics for Kids. The book has its pros and cons. Read the review to find out if your needs will be met.

item.title: Empowering the future: vocational skills in NEP 2020
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Mrunal Raut

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 has emerged as a landmark reform in India's education system, aiming to transform learning from the traditional, theory-heavy model into a holistic andskill-oriented experience. Among its many progressive provisions, the policy's strong emphasis on vocational education stands out as a vital step towards building a skilled and self-reliant nation.

Recognizing the long-standing disconnect between academic knowledge and employability, NEP 2020 envisions an education system that equips students not only with theoretical knowledge but also with practical, real-world skills. It proposes the integration of vocational training from as early as grade 6, with an aim to expose students to various skill-based activities and real-life work environments through internships and hands-on experiences.

A new approach to learning

Unlike the conventional approach where vocational training was offered only at the highersecondary or post-secondary level, NEP 2020 introduces skill-based learning in middle school itself. This early exposure helps students identify their interests and aptitudes at a formative age. The policy advocates for at least one vocational course during grades 6 to 8, supplemented by a 10-day internship with local vocational experts such as carpenters, gardeners, potters, artisans, and others.

In addition, NEP 2020 aims to remove the stigma traditionally associated with vocational education. By giving it equal importance as academic subjects and integrating it into the formal curriculum, the policy promotes dignity of labour and encourages students to pursue skill-based careers without

hesitation.

A diverse skill ecosystem

The vocational courses under NEP 2020 are designed to be both locally relevant and nationally significant. Schools are encouraged to collaborate with local craftsmen, NGOs, industry partners, and community members to offer skills such as:

  • Electric work and carpentry
  • Coding and computer literacy
  • Gardening and organic farming
  • Tailoring and embroidery
  • Pottery and handicrafts
  • Entrepreneurship and business basics

These skills are not just vocational in the traditional sense but also include emerging 21stcentury competencies, preparing students for jobs that are rapidly evolving in today's digital and global economy.

Building a skilled nation

The integration of vocational skills within the mainstream education system is expected to yield far-reaching benefits:

  • Enhanced employability: Students will leave school with tangible skills that increase their chances of getting jobs or starting their own ventures.
  • Reduced dropout rates: Engaging, practical education is more likely to retain students who may

otherwise lose interest in rote learning.

  • Entrepreneurial spirit: With the right exposure, students are more likely to explore self-employment and innovation-driven careers.
  • Self-reliant India: The vision of "Atmanirbhar Bharat" gets a strong boost through the creation of a skilled workforce aligned with national and global demands.

The road ahead

For the successful implementation of vocational education, capacity building at the institutional level is crucial. This includes teacher training, development of flexible and dynamic curricula, infrastructural support, and a collaborative effort between government bodies, industries, and educational institutions.

The vocational education framework prescribed in NEP 2020 is not merely an educational reform,it is a cultural shift. It aspires to create a generation that respects all forms of work, values practical knowledge, and is prepared for the real challenges of life and work.

By reimagining the role of education in shaping a well-rounded individual, NEP 2020 brings us astep closer to a future-ready India, where every child is empowered not just to learn, but also to create, innovate, and contribute meaningfully to society.

Mrunal Raut is Academic in-charge, Pioneer Public School Solapur, Maharashtra.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mrunal Raut
STEAM subjects are not the only subjects one needs to learn if they are to succeed in life. Recognizing this, NEP 2020 has highlighted the importance of vocational skill training to integrate theory with practice and transform children into complete individuals.

item.title: The need to understand the essence of mathematics
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
R. L. Priyadarshini

As a biomedical engineer turned mathematics teacher, I have witnessed the profound role that mathematics plays in every breakthrough, from life-saving equipment to artificial intelligence. A study by the National Academy of Sciences states that mathematics plays a critical role in almost every aspect of modern science and engineering (NAS, 2020,The Role of Mathematics in Modern Science and Engineering).

Despite its obvious importance, mathematicsseems to fill students with fear, and is rarely appreciated for its artistic and creativity nature.

As mathematics teachers and educators, we must acknowledge that our classrooms can seem boring to students because we have not presented mathematics in the way it was perceived by our ancestors. They used mathematics to solve real-life problems without even realizing they were using the subject we now call "mathematics." Unfortunately, in today's classrooms, this real-world connection is not being highlighted, as students are often forced to memorize formulas and theorems instead of exploring the subject’s deeper purpose.

Even as a student of biomedical engineering, I have used mathematical concepts such as integral and differential calculus, vector algebra, and mathematical modelling to model biological systems and design medical devices. But it was when I started teaching mathematics as a part-time teacher that I first realized the basic application of mathematics and how this is not being highlighted in Indian schools.

Visiting countries like Singapore and the United States gave me another perspective towards mathematics. Students in these countries perceive mathematics by connecting it with practical applications. They use calculators to calculate, as it simplifies their work. When I studied at NTU, Singapore, where I did my MS in biomedical engineering, the question papers of mathematics based subjects like biophysics or biomechanics had formulas printed as well for use by the exam-writing students. This is  that students don’t have to memorize and remember formulas. It is not the students memory power that the institute wanted to test but our ability to apply our learning to real-life challenges.

The most innovative countries, like Switzerland, Sweden, and the USA, owe much of their advancements to STEM fields, particularly mathematics (Global Innovation Index, 2020). This also makes the careers in STEM fields grow at a more rapid pace. So, students and parents must ensure that they learn mathematics by understanding that it will empower them to survive and lead in a data-driven futuristic world.

As a former biomedical engineer turnedmathematics teacher, I firmly believe that the future will be shaped by those who understand the essence of mathematics and apply it to solve global problems. I am looking forward to that exciting future and your constructive suggestions to make mathematics a piece of cake.

R. L. Priyadarshini is currently a faculty member at Delhi Public School, Coimbatore, and a passionate theatre artist. She performs in progressive plays with groups like Chennai KalaiKuzhu and was formerly a freelance faculty member at T.I.M.E Institute, Chennai. She can be reached at priyadarshini.rl@dpscoimbatore.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
R L Priyadarshini
One of the most common phobias that you hear of is the fear of mathematics. Unfortunately, a lot of life is based on mathematics and therefore our understanding of the subject is essential. The one way to do this is to help students see the connection between the subject and real life.

item.title: Rhymes, rhymes one more time
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Amita Saundattikar

Rhymes are very useful for students. They help them perceive sounds, pronounce words and learn how to speak. They can also support the development of a number of skills:

*Rhymes teach children the sounds of language and the construction of words. Children can increase their vocabulary and reading proficiency by doing this.

*Rhymes can aid in children’s cognitive development by teaching them how to solve problems.

*Rhymes teach kids about emotions and how to express them, which will aid in their social-emotional development.

*Rhymes can also foster a strong link between children, teachers, or other adults while also assisting children to unwind and de-stress.

Apart from the popular English rhymes, rhymes in vernacular languages are also adored by kids. They are frequently employed to assist kids in learning about numbers,colours, and other ideas. They can aid kids in improving their memory and language while also being a lot of fun to sing.In short, rhymes increase interest in music and the ability to sing songs and simply help kids in their early literacy and reading skills. All teachers should conduct rhymes session every day to grab the attention of the children and make classesmore interesting and enjoyable.

AmitaSaundattikar is a pre-primary facilitator, Pawar Public School, Nanded. She can be reached at amitasaundattikar@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Amita Saundattikar
Why is it that anywhere in the world, little children are taught rhymes? What is it about rhymes that gives them their universal appeal? Let's find out.

item.title: Helping young readers
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Govinda Menaka

In today’s fast-paced, tech-filled world, it’s no surprise that reading doesn’t always come easy for kids. Between smart phones, social media, and the pressure to fit in, young readers are navigating a lot. If we want to help them fall in love with books—and stick with them—we need to understand what they’re looking for and what might be standing in their way.

What do kids want in a book?

Today’s readers aren’t just looking for any story—they want books that feel real to them, stories that reflect their lives, personalities, and even their dreams. Here’s what tends to draw them in:

Representation that feels true

Kids want to see characters that look like them, live like them, or come from families like theirs. When books reflect a child’s identity, they feel seen and valued (Bishop, 1990).

A sense of adventure

From fantasylands to real-world adventures, stories that whisk kids away from their everyday lives give them a place to dream big (Nikolajeva, 2014).

Humour and playfulness

Funny books are a hit—especially ones with silly characters, clever dialogue, or awkward moments that make kids laugh out loud (Lukens et al., 2013).

Relatable struggles

Whether it’s dealing with a best friend fallout or a tough day at school, stories that show kids facing real problems can offer both comfort and insight (Trelease, 2013).

Visuals that pop

Graphic novels, illustrated chapter books, and picture books still rule the shelves. The mix of visuals and storytelling keeps kids engaged—especially those who are visual learners (Short, 2011).

Books that inspire

Kids are drawn to stories that show courage, kindness, and resilience. These books help them believe they can be strong, too (Applegate & Applegate, 2010).

The challenges kids face with reading

Even kids who want to read might struggle to stick with books that are just right for their age. Some common hurdles include:

Trying to fit in

Peer pressure plays a big role. Some kids pick up books that seem “cool” or more mature, even if they aren’t quite ready for them yet (Guthrie & Wigfield, 2000).

Reading level gaps

Not every child reads at the same level, and that’s okay. But it can feel discouraging if a book is too tough—or too easy (Allington, 2012).

Screens everywhere

With video games, YouTube, and social media always a tap away, slower-paced reading can feel less exciting in comparison (Rideout, 2017).

Trying to seem older

Kids often want to “read up” to seem more grown-up. But jumping into books with mature themes too soon can leave them confused or overwhelmed (Galda et al., 2010).

Missing the connection

Sometimes the book just doesn’t click. When kids can’t relate to the characters or storyline, their interest fades (Ivey & Johnston, 2013).

How we can support young readers

So, how do we help? The good news: there’s a lot we can do to help kids find joy in reading—and stick with it.

Offer a variety of genres

Let them try mysteries, sci-fi, graphic novels, poetry—whatever catches their eye. Exploring different types of books keeps things exciting (Tunnell & Jacobs, 2017).

Help without hovering

Support struggling readers with tools like audio books or read-along apps, but let them set the pace. Confidence builds when reading feels doable (Allington & Gabriel, 2012).

Take the pressure off

Let them know it’s okay to read books that match where they are, not where they think they should be. Progress looks different for every kid (Trelease, 2013).

Make it a judgment-free zone

Create a space—at home or in the classroom—where all reading is celebrated. Whether it’s a chapter book or a comic, if they’re reading, they’re winning (Miller, 2009).

Use tech to your advantage

Instead of fighting screens, use them. E-books, apps like Epic!, or audio books with sound effects can make stories come alive (Rideout, 2017).

Conclusion

Helping kids become readers isn’t just about handing them the “right” books—it’s about meeting them where they are. When we give them stories they care about, let them grow at their own pace, and cheer them on; no matter what they’re reading, amazing things can happen. With patience, encouragement, and a little creativity, we can help the next generation fall in love with books—and stay in love for life.

References

Allington,R.L.(2012).What really matters for struggling readers: Designing research-based programs (3rd ed.). Pearson.

Allington, R. L., & Gabriel, R. E. (2012). Every child, every day. Educational Leadership, 69(6), 10–15. Applegate, A.J.,& Applegate, M.D.(2010). Motivating reading comprehension: Concept-oriented reading instruction. Guilford Press.

Bishop, R.S.(1990).Mirrors, windows, and sliding glass doors. Perspectives: Choosing and Using Books for the Classroom, 6(3), ix–xi.

Galda, L., Cullinan, B.E.,&Sipe,L.R.(2010). Literature and the child (8thed.).Wadsworth.

Guthrie, J.T., &Wigfield, A.(2000). Engagement and motivation in reading. Handbook of Reading Research, 3, 403–422.

Ivey, G.,& Johnston, P.H.(2013).Engaged reading as a collaborative competence. Journal of Literacy Research, 45(4), 433–454.

Lukens, R.J., Smith, J.M., & Coffel, C.M.(2013).  A critical handbook of children’s literature (9thed.). Pearson.

Miller, D.(2009). The book whisperer: Awakening the inner reader in every child. Jossey-Bass.

Nikolajeva, M.(2014). Reading for learning: Cognitive approaches to children’s literature. John Benjamins Publishing.

Rideout, V.(2017). The CommonSense Census: Media use by kids age zero to eight. CommonSense Media. Short, K. G. (2011). Literature as a way of knowing. Language Arts, 89(1), 9–16.

Trelease, J.(2013). The read-aloud handbook (7thed.). Penguin Books.

Tunnell, M.O., & Jacobs, J.S. (2017).Children’s literature, briefly (7thed.).Pearson.

Govinda Menaka is a librarian at Udgam School for Children, Thaltej. Passionate about fostering a love for reading among young learners, she is dedicated to curating inclusive and engaging library experiences. Her interests include children’s literature, literacy development, digital literacy, and promoting independent learning through creative library programs and safe reading spaces. She can be reached at menaka2494@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Govinda Menaka
In this age of screens, small and big, how do we get our children start and stick with reading?

item.title: Start with a hook: the art of introduction in concept-based learning
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Vattam Kalpana

How do you transform a seemingly abstract mathematical concept into an exciting and memorable experience for primary and middle schoolers? The answer lies not in the blackboard, but in the buzz of a classroom solving a riddle — a puzzle wrapped in mystery, numbers, and just a hint of your own birthday.

The icebreaker that sparked inquiry

As I entered my classroom, I was committed to doing things differently from the "chalk and talk" method. I aimed not only to present the idea but also to provide a platform for one of the fundamental mathematical concepts: prime numbers. Therefore, rather than a straightforward approach, I gave my students a challenge:

“My birth date is a two-digit number, less than 30 but more than 25. The sum of its digits is a prime number. Can you guess it?

The students were immediately captivated. Giggles, hushed computations, and willing hands filled the air. The problem became a group effort, creating not only numbers, but also friendships in the classroom. Curiosity overcame apprehension.

Learning by discovery: prime numbers demystified

After cracking the birth date, I moved on to discussing prime numbers — those intriguing numbers that are only divisible by one and themselves. We then played a game:

"If your birth date is a prime number, stand up! But don't tell us — give us a riddle, a clue, or a historical connection that allows us to figure it out."

And then there was a lovely moment of bonding. One eager student jumped up and declared:

"My birthday is on Teacher's Day!"

The whole class answered in unison— September 5th! It provided a space for interdisciplinary reflection. We stopped to reflect on Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan and the principles of education, respect, and contribution. This served to create an emotional connection with the lesson and introduce a national and cultural context to numbers. It was no longer merely math — it was life combined with learning.

A mnemonic to remember: PRIME = Product of One, Myself

In order to aid in grounding the idea, I offered a light-hearted mnemonic:

PR – Product of

I – One

ME – Myself

This sentence captured the defining characteristic of prime numbers — that they are independent and not divisible by anything else other than 1 and themselves. It also triggered reflection in the students: isn't it great that we too are one-of-a-kind?

This lesson was not merely about figures. It complemented perfectly our school's teaching, learning, and lesson planning policy, which prioritized:

*Engagement through inquiry

*Inclusivity and differentiated learning

*Reflection and link to real-life contexts

*Cross-curricular links — from maths, to history, and values education

Puzzles enable students to build knowledge, not merely take it in. They provide a level-playing field in which all learners — no matter their speed or style — have a say. And above all, they promote enjoyment of learning.

Vattam Kalpana is Deputy Head for the primary school at Suchitra Academy, Hyderabad. She can be reached at kalpana.vattam@suchitra.in
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vattam Kalpana
Mathematical concepts are abstract and therefore difficult for children to understand, which is why it is important that we catch their attention and interest first.

item.title: Where learning meets play
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
S Anisha Dhinesh Kumar

Let’s face it — in today’s digital-first world, old-school lectures and chalkboards just aren’t cutting it anymore. Students want more than notes and PowerPoints — they want interaction, engagement, and a little bit of fun along the way. That’s where gamification steps in.

Simply put, gamification is the art of adding game-like elements — think points, badges, challenges, or competitions — to the learning process. And it’s not just about fun. Studies show it can boost motivation, improve focus, and make lessons more memorable.

So, how do we bring that magic into the classroom? Here are some of the most popular gamification tools that are making learning exciting in educational institutions around the world:

1. Kahoot! – learning with a buzz

A favourite among students and teachers alike, Kahoot! is a fast-paced quiz game where everyone gets to play — using their own devices.

Why students love it: Real-time questions, cool music, and that thrilling leaderboard!
Why teachers love it: It’s perfect for quick assessments and keeping everyone on their toes.
Try this: Kick off the weekend with a “Friday Kahoot!” to review the week’s lessons.

2. Quizizz – memes + MCQs = magic

If Kahoot! is a quiz show, Quizizz is the fun cousin. It lets students answer questions at their own pace and rewards them with hilarious memes along the way.

Why students love it: It’s relaxed, meme-filled, and doesn’t feel like a test.
Why teachers love it: Great for homework or low-stress classroom activities.

3. Classcraft – turning learning into an adventure

Imagine turning your class into a fantasy role-playing game. With Classcraft, students become heroes who earn points for participation, collaboration, and completing assignments.

Why students love it: They feel like they’re part of a real quest.
Why teachers love it: It encourages good behaviour, teamwork, and goal setting.
Best for: Group projects and boosting classroom dynamics.

4. Blooket – games that teach

Blooket takes learning and mixes it with fun arcade-style games. Students answer questions to progress in mini-games like racing or tower defence.

Why it works: It feels like a game but sneaks in revision and repetition.
Use it for: Competitive reviews, exam prep, or just to make Mondays more fun!

5. Flip (formerly Flip grid) – show what you know on camera

With Flip, students respond to topics by recording short, creative videos. It’s a brilliant tool for presentations, reflections, or even peer feedback.

Why students love it: It's social, personal, and feels familiar — like a school-safe TikTok.
Why educators love it: Great for boosting communication skills and student voice.

6. Breakout EDU – escape room meets education

This one’s for puzzle lovers! Breakout EDU turns your class into a live-action escape room where students work together to solve academic challenges and “break out.”

Why students love it: It’s hands-on, high-energy, and seriously fun.
Why teachers love it: Perfect for review days or building problem-solving skills.

Why try gamification in your class?

  • It grabs attention. Interactive tasks break the monotony and keep students focused.
  • It builds community. Many tools foster teamwork and healthy competition.
  • It supports deeper learning. Game-based repetition helps information stick.
  • It inspires confidence. Earning points and rewards builds positive reinforcement.

Final thoughts

Gamification isn’t just a trend or a buzzword. It’s a way to rethink the learning experience — to make it more student-centered, engaging, and effective.

Whether you’re a seasoned educator or just getting started, adding a little gamification can go a long way in creating a classroom that students actually look forward to.

So why not give it a try? Mix in some quizzes, level-ups, badges, or a little friendly rivalry — and watch your classroom come to life.

S Anisha Dhinesh Kumar is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Computer Science at Jeppiaar College of Arts and Science, where she teaches a range of subjects across BCA and BSc (CS) programs. With a strong academic background, she holds an M.Phil. in Computer Science from the University of Madras and a Master’s degree in Information Technology, complemented by a Bachelor’s degree in Software Application. Anisha has demonstrated excellence throughout her academic journey, earning university ranks, merit scholarships, and top ranks in programming languages such as C++ and Java. Her research expertise lies in the application of machine learning and deep learning techniques, particularly in skin lesion detection, with multiple publications in reputed journals and presentations at national and international conferences. Beyond research, she actively contributes to academic and institutional development by mentoring student projects, participating in faculty development programs, and has held key roles such as ICT Academy Coordinator, ED Cell member, and NSDC course trainer. Additionally, Anisha serves as an exam evaluator for the University of Madras and is committed to enhancing student skills and career readiness through placement cell activities and leadership training. Her dedication to teaching, research, and community engagement marks her as a dynamic and influential educator in the field of computer science. She can be reached at anishakani014@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anisha Dhinesh Kumar
Tech-tools that aid in teaching are aplenty. If you are new and are wondering what apps can help you bring excitement in the classroom, here is a short list to help you get going.

item.title: CyberCare: building a cyber-safe future, one student at a time
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Aarav Shah

As a class of eighth graders chat excitedly over an online game, halfway across the virtual realm, a teenager unknowingly becomes the victim of phishing. In this digital era, scenes like these are increasingly common and deeply impactful. With the rapid evolution of technology, a lack of cyber awareness is leaving many young students vulnerable to online threats ranging from game addiction to cyberbullying and beyond.

Recognizing the urgent need for intervention, Himalay Mondal from Konnagar, West Bengal, decided to act. Two years ago, while still a high school student, Himalay founded CyberCare*, a student-led voluntary organization. Born out of a personal realization about how easily young people could stray into the tangled web of cybercrimes due to sheer unawareness, CyberCare is dedicated to safeguarding students and building a resilient digital community.

"When my friend lost money to an online scam, it struck me how little we knew about protecting ourselves on the internet," recalls Himalay Mondal, founder of CyberCare. "I wanted to change that—to empower us by spreading knowledge."

CyberCare conducts widespread awareness programs directly targeting students from grades 7 to 12 across schools in India. Their efforts extend to influential community gatherings such as book fairs and science expos, using these platforms to extend the reach of their message.

Equipped with projectors and interactive audiovisual presentations, CyberCare’s approach is both engaging and informative. Using real-world examples and poignant case studies, they capture the fluctuating attention spans of young minds. From highlighting the pitfalls of in-game chats to addressing the sinister side of social media misuse, CyberCare’s seminars expose the naked truths of modern cyber threats.

"In one of our sessions, we showed how seemingly harmless in-game interactions could lead to severe privacy invasions," says Sandip Parhar, a volunteer who regularly contributes to these sessions. "The kids were shocked, but that's when learning happens."

Discussing the impact of cyber threats, CyberCare emphasizes the mental health repercussions, from anxiety to isolation, and the importance of staying informed about these dangers. The emotional weight of cyberbullying is a core topic, revealing how damaging it can be to mental health and overall wellbeing.

At its heart, CyberCare is much more than just another educational initiative. It is a grassroots movement aiming to cultivate a proactive mindset among the youth, ensuring digital resilience in a world dominated by technology. It restores confidence, encouraging youngsters to harness technology effectively rather than fearfully.

"Technology is a powerful tool, not an enemy," Himalay often concludes. "Our mission is to teach students to use it wisely and safely." Through CyberCare, Himalay and his team are not only nurturing informed digital citizens but are also sowing seeds of trust and respect between humans and technology, one classroom at a time.

* People can get in touch with CyberCare at cybercare100@gmail.com.

Aarav Shah is a high school sophomore with a strong interest in technology, cybersecurity, and digital responsibility. He serves as a media ambassador for CyberCare, helping raise awareness about online safety among youth. He can be reached at magnifed1234@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aarav Shah
Technology is all around us and youngsters these days are living in virtual worlds. When they spend most of their time with their devices, it is important that they are aware of the pitfalls too. And this is where CyberCare will help.

item.title: Human skills: currency of the future
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Renata Sguario

Let’s get straight to the point: if our students can’t communicate, collaborate, or handle pressure, they won’t thrive—no matter how technically skilled they are.

We’re living through a monumental shift in what it means to be work-ready. Automation is accelerating. AI is transforming industries. And while technical knowledge still matters, it’s the human skills—resilience, self-awareness, problem-solving—that are becoming the true differentiators.

The World Economic Forum calls them the top skills for 2025. McKinsey data shows they form the bulk of foundational job readiness. And here, in India, a staggering 91% of employers now say they’re more important than hard skills. But here’s the catch: fewer than 30% of institutions teach them in any structured way.

So where does that leave us? We’re standing at a powerful intersection of possibility and responsibility.

We’ve underestimated the power of soft skills... until now

A McKinsey Global Survey (2022) shows that 43 of the 56 foundational employability skills are human-centric. And yet, most education systems remain focused on hard content and technical performance, not life readiness.

The result? A deep and growing disconnect between academic outcomes and workplace expectations.

In India, this gap is especially urgent. The Mercer | Mettl Indian Graduate Skill Index 2025 shows that only 42.6% graduates are employable—largely due to poor performance in soft skills. That’s not a pipeline issue. It’s a preparation issue.

Building work-ready humans at scale

At Maxme, we’re responding with purpose. We’ve just launched two new programs in India: ShiftU and IGNIS—tech-enabled, research-backed, and deeply human solutions designed to build capability, confidence, and career-readiness.

ShiftU is an immersive bootcamp experience for high school students. In just 2–3 days, we help young people discover their strengths, handle pressure, build confidence, and begin designing their futures.

It’s high impact, high empathy, and -- perhaps most importantly -- delivered without putting extra pressure on our already stretched educators. We bring the facilitators, we bring the tools, and we work inside the school.

IGNIS is our university program -- a hybrid model that blends digital learning on our Hodie app with real-time coaching. It’s scalable, customisable, and delivered in formats (and tailoring) that fit within academic calendars. Whether students engage for one year or three, they build critical skills that matter: leadership, collaboration, critical thinking, cultural awareness.

And yes—they graduate with a Maxme certification that signals readiness to the workforce.

We’re not replacing the curriculum -- we’re enhancing it
India’s National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 outlines a bold and holistic vision for the future of education. We’re proud that our programs align directly with its goals:

  • Multidisciplinary learning
  • 21st century skill development
  • Bridging academia and employability
  • Experiential and values-based education

Human skills are not “soft” - they’re strategic

Let’s stop calling them soft skills. They’re the hard currency of future careers. And in this new age of AI, the very skills that make us human—empathy, adaptability, creativity—are what make us most valuable.

So, what comes next?

We’re here to empower. Our work is about democratizing access to personal growth, career clarity, and confidence -- so every student, from every background, has the tools to thrive.

This is a call to action for educators, institutions, and policymakers: Let’s make human skills core business in education. Let’s build not just smarter students, but stronger humans -- ones who are creative and innovative, with empathy and self-awareness. Because, if we truly want to prepare our young people for the world ahead, it starts with what’s already inside them. We need to give them the tools to bring it out.

To bring ShiftU or IGNIS to your institution, visit www.maxme.com.au/india or email explore@maxme.com.au.

Renata Sguario had a successful corporate career for 30 years. She has now started Maxme – an organization that focuses on the critical, yet often overlooked, importance of human (soft) skills.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Renata Sguariio
Did you know that in India only 42.6% graduates are employable? It is not the subject that these students lack in, but the soft skills. It is time that our educational institutions took up training our students in empathy, adaptability, and creativity as well along with the math, science, and language.

item.title: Why interdisciplinary approach in the field of education: the story of my school
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Harshit Rana

Education is not static, it keeps evolving. According to BW Education (2022), over 80% of schools in India — especially in rural areas — still rely on traditional teaching methods, which are, more often than not, teacher-led. But, if education is evolving with time, there is a need for the teaching methods to evolve too. Schools must adopt a holistic and interdisciplinary pedagogic approach to become vibrant and thriving learning spaces.

My school, Nankamatta Public School (NPS) came into existence around 2012. In the beginning, it was just an ordinary school, dominated by traditional methods of teaching, where students were lifeless and productivity was almost dead. Until 2019, this outdated system continued. There was no space for creativity and innovation.

In 2020, when COVID-19 was all around us, a miracle happened — a turning point in the history of NPS: the introduction of the Deewar Patrika (wall magazine). The credit for this idea goes to our mentor, Mahesh Punetha.

This initiative became a spark of change. I remember the first Deewar Patrika that came out with the help of our senior students. It gave us a chance to explore the world of writing. Initially, it was a tough journey — we never thought something like this could be part of our school learning. But slowly, we realized that education is not just textbooks. Real learning happens through experience and expression.

Deewar Patrika opened the doors to many new possibilities. Though COVID-19 killed millions across the country, we were living in a rural area of Uttarakhand and somehow managed to continue our learning. We believe learning never dies.
