AI in classrooms is no longer a distant future, it is already unfolding. Yet, democratizing AI education is no small feat, significant challenges remain to implementing AI literacy at scale.

Different policies, programmes, and investments are running parallel under the Indian government to create an AI-ready education system. Let’s take the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, for example, which mandates the integration of 21st century skills including AI in the curricula. The government has allocated ₹500 crore in the Union Budget for FY 2025-26 to institutionalize a Centre of Excellence in AI for Education, focusing on personalized learning, skilling, and workforce readiness. At the state level, Telangana has launched Shiksha Copilot in partnership with Microsoft Research India and Sikshana Foundation to provide personalized lesson plans for heterogeneous classrooms.

Apart from the governments, not-for-profit and philanthropic actors are also actively working to bring AI education to schools. The Central Square Foundation’s AI Samarth, in partnership with the Wadhwani School of Data Science & AI at IIT Madras, is building a comprehensive AI literacy framework that aims to reach 5 million students, parents, and educators within 3–4 years.

It is safe to say that India has moved beyond asking, “Should we teach AI in schools?” The bigger question is, “How do we remove barriers to an AI-literate nation?” The answer lies in confronting four pressing challenges that continue to hold back the vision of AI-ready classrooms.

1. Access to devices and connectivity

Digital access is the foundation for AI education. But significant gaps persist. Only 57.2% of schools have functional computers, and 53.9% have internet access. At the household level, over 95% of Indian households have a telephone or mobile phone, but just 9.9% own a computer. In households with digital access, devices are often shared among family members, limiting their availability for students. Furthermore, ASER’s 2024 report indicates that only 57% students use smartphones for educational purposes. This makes scaling AI literacy a highly uneven journey for students from low-income backgrounds.

2. Inclusive and holistic curriculum

AI is being rolled out in stages, but gaps remain. CBSE offers AI as a skill subject from class IX onwards, with a 15-hour foundational module available from class VI in some schools. Technical depth is confined mainly to secondary and higher secondary levels, while younger students receive only a conceptual introduction. There is no universally adopted, all-grades curriculum with consistent technical rigour across school boards or states. In contrast, Western models often integrate age-appropriate AI exposure from primary to senior levels. India’s fragmented approach risks creating unequal readiness.

3. Student data and privacy

AI literacy is about responsible use. AI in schools could expose children to risks of data misuse and surveillance without proper data security measures. Karnataka’s plan to use AI-based face recognition for school attendance triggered alarms about student privacy. Education apps like DIKSHA have previously exposed sensitive teacher and student data due to server misconfigurations. Schools need robust mechanisms to protect student data and prevent misuse while integrating AI in curriculums.

4. AI’s impact on critical thinking

Educators must promote the idea of responsible AI usage among students. Emerging research indicates that excessive AI usage can adversely impact students' higher-order thinking skills when used as a substitute for critical thinking. Students must be made aware of both the pros and cons of using AI. The AI curriculum should provide students with the tools to identify ethical and right use of AI with real-life applications.

India is making meaningful strides toward preparing students for an AI-enabled world. Yet, oversights remain: patchy device access, inadequate privacy safeguards, and fragmented curricula could leave many students behind. If these gaps can be addressed proactively, AI literacy will become widespread and equitable. The next generation of school-goers will then not only be AI-ready when they enter the workforce but also capable of shaping AI responsibly and inclusively.

Shruti Sharma is a public policy student at Takshashila Institution and a former Teach For India Fellow. Over the past three years, she has worked across classrooms and data operations, exploring how education and policy can come together to create meaningful change. She can be reached atShruti100sharma10@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shruti Sharma
AI is the future and reconginzing its importance the Indian government and a few other actors, including NGOs, are launching programmes to get our students, the citizens of tomorrow, ready for an AI world. But if we do not want to fall behind other countries, we have to overcome a few challenges.

item.title: The TANMAYA teacher…an agent of positive change
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar

Every child has potential, all they need is for someone to recognize this potential and give them direction. But, do we have teachers who can do this? There’s no simple answer to this question. For, teachers are part of an ecosystem that keeps them bound-- syllabus completion, little or no freedom to try experimental teaching techniques, indifferent school leadership, etc. Students belong to the future. Without access to a time machine or a crystal ball that can predict the future, a teacher has to get her students “future-ready”. This is not an easy task at all.

The challenge for the teacher is tremendous. The resources and environment available to the teacher may or may not have the scope for innovation, personalization, or experimentation, but still the teacher can become a change-agent. We have all had that one teacher who made us feel special and left an impact on us in more ways than one. It may have been a kind word or a simple ‘star’ in the notebook. Self-reflection and introspection can transform any teacher from merely being a source of information to becoming a transformational change-agent in the lives of her students.

Transformational teachers as change-agents

A transformational teacher is one who not only transforms her students, but also is willing to transform herself. Transformational teachers are educators who inspire and motivate their students to achieve their highest potential and foster a deep love of learning. These teachers go beyond traditional teaching roles to create a positive, engaging, and supportive learning environment. Teachers can inspire students to overcome obstacles and focus on their strengths.

Once they become aware of the power they have to transform, teachers can sow the seeds of change in the fertile minds of their students.

The TANMAYA teacher

The word TANMAYA means ‘wholly absorbed’. A “TANMAYA teacher” is someone who is completely absorbed in their students’ all-round development.

The key to transformational teaching is an intentional choice to focus on learners and learning and designing learning experiences to meet them where they are and help them grow into unique and special individuals. The impact of a teacher who sees students as individuals, who gets to know them, and cultivates their creativity and curiosity while developing academic skills is hard to measure. The TANMAYA teacher’s goal is to inspire students to become the best version of themselves in all aspects of life. Self awareness, self belief, self confidence, self development, and self expression are the milestones of the journey of a student in the classroom of a TANMAYA teacher. By fostering a positive, engaging, and supportive learning environment, they help students develop the skills, knowledge, and mindset needed for lifelong success.

Classroom practices of TANMAYA teachers

  1. Creating a safe environment
  2. Encouraging student participation
  3. Praising good practices
  4. Connecting their teaching to the real-world
  5. Celebrating individual differences
  6. Embracing the growth mindset
  7. Building positive relationships with students
  8. Facilitating active learning

As the world moves from traditional forms of education to more student-centric models, the impact of a teacher will depend on the intentional choices he or she makes in the classroom and beyond. So long as students interact with teachers in the process of learning, there is scope for a teacher to become a change-agent. The TANMAYA teacher is not merely focused on academic excellence in the present. Building a strong character, a moral compass, self-regulation, the ability to delay gratification, and above all, the wisdom to make choices about what to focus on in life are some of the intangible gifts of a TANMAYA teacher to the keen learner in the class.

The concept of TANMAYA Teacher is an original idea @ Dr. Pragya Mathur Kuamr,

Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar is a psychologist, trainer, and founder ArunimaAnkuran. She can be reached atarunimaankuran@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar
While we all agree that students have to be ready for an unknown future, what kind of teacher do we need to help our students get ready for that future?

item.title: Are exams still the best way to measure student success?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Manisha Sharma

As classrooms grapple with rising levels of student stress and a workforce that values adaptability over rote learning, the value of academic performance is coming under scrutiny. The traditional metrics, standardized exams, and rigid grading systems, which are used to measure student success, often overlook the skills that are required to thrive in the real world. In this rapidly changing world, we must look at student success differently and rethink how we can measure their capabilities.

This article will explore how to navigate the transforming landscape of education and rethink how we assess a student’s calibre.

Beyond academic report cards

A school curriculum is usually divided into two semesters, which include a lot of examinations. Students are obligated to memorize information and reproduce it in a given frame of time to secure a good grade. These grades are then used to determine whether a student is capable of being successful or not. High grades are often equated with success and low grades bring the stigma of underachievement—a life of failure. This narrow-minded approach needs a dire change and requires an outlook that doesn’t judge a student’s capabilities by their scores.

The real world is not looking for employees adept at textbook knowledge. Employers are increasingly looking for adaptable, flexible, curious individuals who can think critically, communicate efficiently, and have the capability to learn on the go. Meanwhile, students are also navigating rising stress levels and the need for more purpose-driven learning. In this context, relying solely on grades can restrict the ways a student can grow, learn, and contribute to society.

Redefining success for students

To prepare students for a life beyond the classroom, educational institutions must redefine and evolve their perspective of a student’s success. Effective education takes into account not just academic performance but also emotional intelligence and social and practical skills. Students can assess themselves better if they are directed towards a balanced approach. Project-based learning, portfolio assessments, peer reviews, and self-evaluations can offer a more nuanced and deeper understanding of a student’s potential. Incorporating feedback from faculty members, mentors, and even the community can help enrich this process.

Holistic development does not mean lowering the standards, it means raising the bar to include a full range of skills and traits that define a compassionate, confident, and smart learner.

Preparing students for tomorrow by enlightening them today

There is a growing hunger for development and advancement in this era, and educational institutions must go beyond the conventional methods of academics to adapt to this modern age of learning. Learning cannot be limited to classrooms and textbooks. It is essential to inculcate practical knowledge, emotional intelligence, and a sense of social responsibility among the students. Institutions have surpassed the traditional ways of teaching and directed their focus to a balanced approach towards education and learning. With initiatives such as internship opportunities for high school students, and developing core life skills through programmes, schools are determined to build a holistic educational environment for their students.

Conclusion

Studying for examinations is certainly crucial. However, it is only one part of a larger picture. At a time when empathy, emotional intelligence, and social responsibility are as important as textbook knowledge, academic platforms should reflect on their significance as well. By moving forward from the narrow academic metrics and adopting a balanced assessment, schools can better prepare students, not just to pass exams, but to lead lives of meaning and impact.

Dr. Manisha Sharma is Principal, Venkateshwar International School, Sector 10,Dwarka. She can be reached at principal@vis10dwarka.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Manisha Sharma
In the 21st century, we need creative and innovative problem-solvers and yet schools still measure student success in therms of their exam results. It is time for us to rethink what student success is and how we can measure it.

item.title: Bridging the cyber security skills gap in India: the role of industry-led education
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Karmendra Kohli

In the ever-evolving world of cyber threats, the skills required to defend against them must evolve just as fast. Yet, many academic programmes still rely on outdated curricula, producing graduates who are not job-ready. As a professional who has long operated at the intersection of industry and education, I believe that closing the cyber security skills gap in India requires robust collaboration between the corporate sector and academic institutions.

At the heart of the issue lies one fundamental truth: education must be industry-aligned. The world of cybersecurity changes rapidly, so course content and outlines must be periodically reviewed and updated. Technologies, attack vectors, and threat landscapes evolve by the week—sometimes even by the day—and it is crucial that the knowledge imparted in our classrooms reflects this dynamism.

We design modular courses that allow us to swiftly integrate new topics, tools, and threat models as they emerge. “Our students at SecurEyes are encouraged to think critically, to ask why something works the way it does. That mindset of questioning is key to staying updated in cybersecurity.

Learning by doing: internships and hands-on labs
A theory-heavy curriculum is not enough. Cybersecurity is a field that demands hands-on experience. Emphasis on live internships, which are offered to students who complete cybersecurity courses, ensures they gain hands-on experience with real-world threats and enterprise environments, effectively bridging the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application.

We also invest heavily in lab infrastructure, enabling students to experiment, simulate attacks, and build defence mechanisms in a controlled environment. This real-time exposure is invaluable and is something no textbook can substitute.

 From curated curriculum to lifelong learning
One of the most important traits we try to instil in our learners is the spirit of self-learning. A cybersecurity graduate must understand that their education does not end with the course. If someone graduates today, their knowledge might be partially outdated in just a few months. That is the nature of this field. It is not about simply finishing a course. It is about cultivating the habit of self-driven learning—an attitude that keeps professionals sharp, current, and relevant.

Effective models of industry-academia collaboration
There are many models that can successfully bridge the academia-industry gap: internships, apprenticeships, curriculum co-creation, boot camps, and even university-industry certification programs. The key is to keep students in touch with the realities of the industry.

Cybersecurity training should follow a model that combines academic depth with real-world industry exposure. Ideally, such programs must be built in collaboration with industry partners who can bring in current, field-tested expertise.

For example, our partnership with universities to launch M.Sc. and M.Tech. programs in Cybersecurity reflects this approach. As knowledge partners, we ensure that students do not just learn theoretical concepts—they are exposed to real-world case studies, attend guest lectures from seasoned cybersecurity professionals, and gain hands-on experience in state-of-the-art lab environments. This kind of immersive, industry-integrated learning is essential to prepare professionals who are ready to handle modern-day cyber challenges from day one.

Beyond the tech: soft skills, ethics, and mind management
While technical training is vital, it is not the only skill cybersecurity professionals need. We cannot forget the human side of cybersecurity. Ethical considerations, soft skills, and a proactive security mindset are just as important as technical prowess.

We focus on training students to develop a positive and disciplined mindset. Mind management techniques, stress control, and even modules on meditation or yoga have found their place in our courses. People need to know how to stay calm and composed, especially when under cyberattack. These are high-stress jobs. If we can build resilient minds along with technical experts, we have done our job right.

Government and corporate collaboration
To truly scale impact, government departments, cybersecurity firms, and universities must work together. There is a massive demand-supply gap in cybersecurity talent, and only a collaborative effort can bridge it.

Measuring success in cybersecurity education
Cybersecurity training should be structured to not only build strong technical foundations but also assess a learner’s ability to apply knowledge in real-world scenarios. It should include rigorous assessments, group discussions, and project presentations, much like what we do in our flagship CSCP program (Cyber Security Certification Program). These elements help evaluate not just technical competence but also communication, critical thinking, and teamwork skills. The ultimate measure of success is employability. If learners are able to crack interviews, secure jobs, and thrive in their roles, that is when we know the training has truly made an impact.

Cybersecurity for all
Cybersecurity is not just for those who want to make a career in it. It is for everyone, from curious students to working professionals seeking to understand how to protect their digital presence.

The future of cybersecurity education lies in adaptability, collaboration, and a holistic approach to learning. Whether you are a student, an educator, or a professional, it is time we all come together to secure our digital future—one skill at a time.

Karmendra Kohli is the CEO & Director, SecurEyes, a pure-play cybersecurity consulting, services, and products company that also provides cybersecurity training and education. He can be reached at karmendra.kohli@secureyes.net.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Karmendra Kohli
In the digital world, cyber threats and attacks are a reality. Knowing about cybercrime and how to handle them is the need of the hour not just for someone who is looking to make a career halting cybercrime but for every person who has some kind of a digital presence.

item.title: Stepwells: a holistic view
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Shalini Sabikhi

A stepwell also known as a vav is an ancient source of water with steps leading down to the water. Stepwells are found in many regions in India, especially in the arid states of Rajasthan and Gujarat. A stepwell is an excellent example of rainwater harvesting followed in India in the ancient times. It provided water for irrigation, cooking, washing, and drinking. The temperature in a stepwell is 50 less than that on the ground. So, it served as a resting place for people, providing them respite from the heat and fruits from the orchard around the stepwell. Religious rituals were performed here and it was also a place to socialize for women. With the passage of time, stepwells fell into neglect.

Now with growing awareness, many stepwells are being revived and also being used for music festivals and photo shoots.

Some of the most famous stepwells in India are:

  • Rani ki Vav in Patan, Gujarat (UNESCO World Heritage site)
  • Adalaj Vav in Gandhinagar, Gujarat
  • Chand Baori in Abhaneri, Rajasthan
  • Dholavira Stepwell in Dholavira, Gujarat
  • Toorji ka Jhalra in Jodhpur, Rajasthan
  • Bai Harir Vav in Ahmedabad, Gujarat

Stepwells can become an excellent source of learning for school students. There is something to learn in each subject from a stepwell. So, a theme exhibition on stepwells will indeed be enlightening and educational. This theme can be announced at the beginning of the academic session (for class7 or above) so that the students get enough time to read, visit, research, and prepare for the exhibition to be held at the end of the year. Field trips to stepwells can also be organized by the school.

For the exhibition, different rooms or spaces can be provided for different subjects.

In Room 1, students can do a role play about how people used stepwells in the past--women would come to fill water and spend time with each other, pilgrims, traders, and travellers would come to quench their thirst, rest, and eat, Some people would conduct religious rituals. Students can share about the culture and society of the past, the importance of water in daily life and how stepwells have helped sustain civilizations.

Room 2 can display charts on various stepwells with pictures and information.

Room 3 can be dedicated to mathematics, where students can share about symmetry in the steps and wells, mathematical formulas for the heights of steps, angles at which they are made, heights of the columns, proportions in the steps, geometric shapes and patterns, etc. 

Room 4 can be for history. Students can talk about ancient Indian engineering and architecture, which include the intricate designs, craftsmanship, and functional use of stepwells. They can also share about the historical periods during which stepwells were constructed and their role in trade, religion, and daily life.

Room 5 can be for environmental awareness, sustainability, and water management.  Understanding how stepwells were built in harmony with Nature will encourage students to consider sustainable practices in modern times. Stepwells were sustainable sources of water ensuring people had enough water throughout the year. They make use of innovative water conservation methods such as storing and managing water during droughts and dry seasons. Students can share about this very important aspect, which is a need of the present times when the groundwater level is receding in many cities. They can share ideas on how to make stepwells functional once again and other innovative methods for rainwater harvesting.

Room 6 for geography. The location of a stepwell depends on the climate, availability of construction materials, and presence of a natural source of water like a river, lake, or underground aquifer. Students can share their learnings in geography from stepwells.

Room 7 for architecture. The multistory designs, often with complex carvings and sculptures, can inspire students to make presentations on architectural innovation and creativity. Students can also share about the arches, columns, carvings, etc. They can make models of stepwells, including the different types named on the basis of the number of entrances.

Room 8 for sociopolitical influence. Stepwells were often commissioned by women or rulers reflecting the political and social conditions of the time. It was a place of interaction between different classes. Students can share why the people then thought that constructing stepwells and providing water was a pious act.

Room 9 for art. Many stepwells have intricate carvings and sculptures, from which students can learn about the artistic skills of the people of the past. They can display sketches of various stepwells. They can share fun facts like how the carvings in Rani ki Vav in Patan are similar to the Patola designs on sarees.

Room 10 for religious significance. Students can share how water has always been sacred for various civilizations. Religious rituals were performed in stepwells and the water was believed to have healing properties. Students can also share about the rituals and local beliefs.

Room 11 for preservation and conservation. Students can share about the importance of conserving historical monuments for future generations. They can also share about how they helped in cleaning up neglected structures as part of community service, try to get the required permissions to sow seeds that will grow into fruit bearing trees, so that the surrounding orchards can be revived and suggest methods by which stepwells can be recharged with water.

Room 12 for tourism and economy. Stepwells are a tourist attraction and they add to the economy. Thus, students can share about the role of tourism on local economies and how they help a number of people earn-- hotels, cafes, tour guides, souvenir shops, local crafts stores, photographers, tour operators, etc.

Room 13 to display photographs that the students clicked on their visit to various stepwells.

Room 14 for showcasing how music festivals are held in stepwells in the present times. Students can put up a classical/folk music and dance show against the backdrop of a stepwell.

Room 15 for books on stepwells. Tie up with a book seller and he can bring various books on stepwells to be browsed and bought.

Such an exhibition will provide a holistic view of what students can learn from stepwells, combining mathematical, historical, cultural, environmental, art, dramatics, music, and architectural lessons.

Book suggestions 

  • The Vanishing Stepwells of India-Victoria Lautman
  • Steps to Water- Morna Livingston
  • To Reach the Source- Claudio Cambon
  • Her Space, Her Story-Purnima Mehta Bhatt
  • Underground Shrine-Jaikishandas Sadani
  • Lesser Known stepwells in and around Ahmedabad-Gandhinagar region-Urban Management Centre

Shalini Sabikhi is a social studies educator in Ahmedabad.  She is interested in reading, travelling, gardening, art and craft, learning and sharing her learnings with others. She can be reached at svs502@yahoo.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shalini Sabhiki
That arch from a bygone era, under which you pass by every day to school, those remnants of a Mughal chieftain's palace , or the tombs of kings and queens, these are not just relics of the past, these monuments can teach us quite a lot and not just history. Shalini Sabikhi shows how stepwells can be a wealth of knowledge.

item.title: Let’s have fun with circle time!
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja

Circle time is a foundational part of early childhood education that nurtures social, emotional, and cognitive development. Through engaging activities like puppet shows, storytelling, rhymes, action songs, and imaginative play, children build essential skills in a joyful and interactive setting. This article explores how to make circle time meaningful and magical by weaving fun and learning together using the world around children as a source of wonder and creativity.


Puppet play
Puppets are an excellent tool for grabbing children's attention and inspiring storytelling. Teachers can create and introduce a “Circle Time Friend” like Milo the Monkey or Tina the Turtle, who greets the children each day and shares little stories or riddles. Puppets can also act out social scenarios, teach values, and encourage children to participate by speaking or responding.
Tip: Use hand puppets, stick puppets, or even sock puppets. Let children take turns being the “puppeteer.”

Stories that spark imagination
Storytelling fuels children’s imagination and vocabulary. During circle time, teachers can read from books, narrate picture-free stories, or even create collaborative tales with inputs from the children.
Activity: Start a story with “Once upon a time, there was a magical tree…” and let the children suggest what happens next. This builds creativity and confidence.

Rhymes and rhythm
Rhymes and chants develop phonemic awareness and memory. Classic rhymes like “Twinkle Twinkle” or action rhymes like “Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes” are perfect for circle time. They also support coordination and language development.
Extension: Add musical instruments like tambourines or maracas for extra fun!

Movement and expression
Children learn best when their whole bodies are engaged. Action songs like “If You’re Happy and You Know It” or “The Hokey Pokey” combine rhythm with gross motor movement, making learning a full-body experience.
Bonus: Create a “movement dice” with actions like jump, wiggle, dance, spin, freeze!

Exploring the world around us
Nature themes, animal movements, weather songs, or daily observations about the environment help children connect with the world. Discuss the season, day of the week, or talk about animals, insects, and flowers.
Activity: Bring a “mystery object” from Nature (like a pinecone or leaf) and let children guess what it is or share what they know about it.

Let imagination soar
Use pretend play and imaginative scenarios. “Let’s fly to the moon today” or “Let’s visit the jungle” are fun ways to transport kids to new adventures. Props like scarves, hats, or masks enhance the experience.

Circle time activities can be brain boosting for small kids and help in cognitive development and critical thinking..Circle time is not only about sitting in a circle but it is about listening to children’s experiences and sharing a lot of amazing things with them.

Sunita Ahuja has been a passionate teacher for about 17 years. Currently, she is working as a pre-school teacher in Reliance Foundation School, Mouda. She can be reached at sunitaahuja700@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja
Circle time is a good way of incorporating some fun into learning. Here are a few activities that you can try with your little ones.

item.title: School politics: the elephant in the room
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Akanksha

They say, “A good teacher is like a candle who consumes itself to light the way for others.“ But what happens when the candle itself struggles to light up and has to depend on others for its existence? Teaching is considered a noble profession, but does this mean that employers can exploit teachers for their own benefits? Teachers, today, are trying to stay afloat in an environment that is anything but noble.

Once upon a time, a passionate and enthusiastic teacher joined her first school with high hopes for a bright career, only to learn that she is jumping into a vicious cycle of jealousy, competition, and insecurity. She got an opportunity to teach the subject she was passionate about and competent in. But her enthusiasm soon faded away. She learnt that her colleagues were not comfortable with her. She was questioned at every step, from creating lesson plans to making notes, so much so that she started doubting her own ability to teach. Frustration began to set in. She tried to reach a higher authority for support, only to find that this is a game of people-pleasing and survival. Her subject was looked down upon and often criticized.

It is not just the students, but teachers are also subjected to the science > commerce > humanities hierarchy. Parents and schools have already labelled some subjects as CORE and others as peripheral. Your value is also determined accordingly. Even within the subject groups there is hierarchy. English teachers look down on the other subjects in the humanities group as being easy to teach and assess, but it is always the English teachers who are in the limelight—they get opportunities to speak at forums on behalf of the school or be the hosts for events in the school. The new teacher was having a tough time. She was being questioned, the papers she set were being rejected, and her classroom management skills were never “up to the mark” and if she dared point out any mistakes made by the senior teachers, she saw their wrath.

She was slowly adjusting to this toxicity, as after all, it is the survival of the fittest, right? Soon, she started realizing that her hard work and dedication was not being recognized as she expected. There was no concept of appraisal as per one’s performance. She was intentionally given lower grades to teach inspite of graduating from one of the most renowned institutions in the country and having all the necessary skills to teach the higher grades. Finally, she decided to leave the school. She joined a “reputed” institution in the city. The first few days were exciting, but soon the vicious cycle of politics started rising its head again. This time it was subtle and refined with multiple layers which can put even corporate politics to shame.

Her patience was being tested every day. She started doubting her career choice. She was not enthusiastic about her job anymore, even though she still loved her subject and students. School politics was draining her; she was unable to develop a thick skin. People-pleasing was not her cup of tea. In the end, she was defeated -- defeated by seniors who could not see her getting the limelight , defeated by colleagues who could only think about their benefit, and defeated by “friends” who were ready to backstab just to please the authority. She resigned with a very heavy heart, paying three months of her salary as per the rule and was blamed by the institution for her impulsive decision. Was this decision impulsive? I leave you to think….

This is not the story of one teacher only. Education has become a business. India used to be a land of gurus who were given the highest respect, but today this profession is reduced to delivering lessons, paper checking, management, and parent-pleasing. It has no soul. It feels no less than a slaughter house of people who choose this profession for the betterment of society and job satisfaction, but end up getting butchered at the hands of people who think they know the real meaning of education.

Akanksha is a freelance educator based in Bengaluru. She graduated in geography (hons.) from the prestigious Miranda House, Delhi University. She has 8 years of teaching experience. Currently, she is working as a geography teacher and UPSC coach in Bengaluru. She is passionate about her subject and is working to develop a good pedagogy and resources for teaching-learning geography. She can be reached at akankshaakki1416@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Akanksha
Teaching they say is a noble profession. But in a sacred environment, when politics makes an entry, dignity, respect, and professionalism make their exit, leaving the noble profession murky.

item.title: Lessons I learned as a teacher
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
R.K. Gayathiri Devi

Teaching is not just a profession for me—it is a journey of continuous learning, self-discovery, and resilience. Over the years, my classroom has been my greatest teacher.

One of the first lessons I learned was that patience is powerful. Working with young minds, especially in early childhood education, requires immense patience. Every child learns at their own pace, and the joy of seeing even the tiniest of progresses has taught me to celebrate every effort.

I also learned that empathy builds strong bridges. As someone who faced challenges—both personal and professional—I understood early on that every student comes with their own story. When we listen, understand, and encourage, we build trust and make learning meaningful.

Creativity became my everyday tool. Limited resources never limited my ideas. I designed projects from simple things, used songs, stories, and even local culture to make learning joyful. Teaching taught me to think out-of-the-box.

Most importantly, I learned never to give up. I once cleared a government exam but lost the appointment due to corruption. It was heartbreaking. But I didn’t let that stop me. I kept studying further, set world records, wrote papers, and continued teaching with even more dedication.

Each child I teach inspires me to be better. And every obstacle I face reminds me that real teaching is about leading by example.

These are the lessons that shape me, not just as a teacher—but as a lifelong learner.

R.K. Gayathiri Devi is a dedicated early childhood educator, gold medalist in law, and a world record holder.  She believes in teaching with heart and continues to inspire young minds through innovative methods and perseverance. She can be reached a rk.gayathri07@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
R K Gayathri Devi
Teachers are expected to teach, but are there lessons that they learn? A teacher writes about the lessons she has learnt.

item.title: What true inclusion looks like
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Anwar Hussain Rayma

New Education Policy 2020 strongly emphasizes an inclusive education in all schools, so that even differently-abled children get a chance to blossom. Currently, how many schools can claim that they are inclusive in the true sense of the term?

I can proudly say that the school I work in is truly an inclusive school, where we help differently-abled children reach their potential. Special educators have a huge role to perform in such cases and in our school we have Mr. Kamran Khavra.

Kamran and I joined Adani Public School in 2010, and over these years, I have witnessed how Kamran has been instrumental in the growth of many of our special students. In this article, I narrate the story of Siddhrajsinh Jadeja, a differently-abled child with vision problems. His story is testimony to our school’s inclusive nature.

Siddhraj’s vision was compromised, but his listening skills were excellent. Siddhraj was in class three when I first noticed that he was able to tell an entire story, word to word, and recite poems soon after I sang them for the class. His classmates too began to notice this ability and they all stood up and clapped for him on one occasion when he summarized a long Gujarati story that was just read out in class. If a school wants to become truly inclusive, all its students (the normal children) too should learn to accept children with different abilities, stand by them and support them.

As Siddhraj reached higher classes and lessons became more complex, Kamran began working with him more closely introducing him to several new learning techniques and sharing materials with him. He also began sitting in Siddhraj’s classes to observe and find out how much of the lessons the boy understood. Kamran trained the rest of Siddhraj’s teachers in methods that helped Siddhraj understand lessons better. With almost the entire school working to support Siddhraj, he became so meticulous that once I remember when Siddhraj was writing a science exam and his scribe had written out the answer, I noticed that it was wrong. It seemed unlikely that Siddhraj could make a mistake like that. So out of curiosity, I read out the question again and asked if his answer was still the same. He immediately said, “Sir, my scribe must have forgotten to add this particular term here as there cannot any other answer.” And it turned out that he was right!

This kind of diligence on the part of both Siddhraj and his teachers helped him graduate his class 10 and 12 exams with grades even better than normal children in some cases-- 82 percent in the 10th and 78 percent in his 12th commerce board exams.

Siddhraj got a special award from the chairman of our institution for his achievements.

Anwar Hussain Rayma is a seasoned educator whose tenure at Adani Public School, Mundra, encompassed a remarkably diverse set of roles. Apart from primary instruction, he served as the In-Charge of Extracurricular Activities, directed plays, and taught social science to classes VI to VIII. Rayma’s practice is fundamentally grounded in child psychology and educational psychology. A dedicated practitioner of activity-based learning, his methodology consistently centred on Bloom's Taxonomy to design engaging, high-order thinking activities for students. He can be reached at anwarrayma209@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anwar Hussain Rayma
Even though inclusion in Indian schools came into focus with the National Education Policy 1986, and has been reinforced upon in every policy after, even today in 2025 not all schools are able to fulfill this requirement. However, there are a few schools who have managed to integrate children with different abilities into the mainstream to achieve true inclusion. Read on to know about one such school.

item.title: Empowering young minds:connecting economics and business education to real-world thinking
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Jyoti Punjwani

As an economics and business teacher working with senior school students, I realize that my subject is not confined to textbooks alone—it is deeply woven into our daily lives. This makes teaching it a privilege, as it equips young learners with the skills they need to understand the world and to prepare for life beyond the classroom.

Economics and business provide students with ample opportunities to think critically, connect classroom learning with real-life experiences, and develop practical solutions to the challenges that individuals, businesses, and economies face today. Whether analyzing global economic trends or discussing everyday financial choices, students learn to think beyond conventional answers and approach problems with creativity and confidence.

One of the most rewarding aspects of teaching this subject is seeing how learners begin to apply their knowledge to their own lives. For instance, when faced with financial challenges, they start to ask: What options do I have? What strategies can I use? In doing so, they are not only developing academic skills but also preparing themselves for real-world decision-making.

Making young learners think critically about real-world economics and business
The main purpose of studying economics and business is not just to understand theories, but to evaluate ideas, question assumptions, analyze real-world events, and reflect on their impact. When my senior school learners first engaged with current affairs, they often viewed them as isolated pieces of news rather than opportunities to think critically. Through structured discussions and guided practice, they gradually began to approach these issues with an analytical mindset.

Starting to interpret and form an opinion
To cultivate the skill of analysis, I regularly bring current news articles into class—for example, “Trump imposes 50% tariff on Indian exports.” With such articles, I guide students through a process of questioning and reasoning:

What is the core issue presented?

Who are the stakeholders and how might each be affected?

How will this decision impact the economy and businesses in the short and long run?

What alternative strategies could the government adopt to reduce negative impacts?

If I were a policymaker or a business owner, what steps would I take?

By debating these questions, learners are encouraged to look at problems from multiple perspectives and develop their own evidence-based opinions.

Why real-world application matters
Engaging with real economic and business issues helps learners connect theory to practice, moving beyond memorization to critical application. They learn how policies and decisions affect trade, employment, and investment. More importantly, they begin to see themselves as problem-solvers who can propose solutions, whether for national-level challenges or personal financial decisions.

This approach not only strengthens their analytical and debating skills but also prepares them for life beyond school. It equips them with the 21st century competencies of critical thinking, adaptability, and decision-making—skills they will carry into their future studies, careers, and personal lives.

Jyoti Punjwani is an economics and business teacher in Inventure Academy, Bengaluru. with a passion for guiding young learners to see how these subjects shape the world around them. She can be reached at jyotipunjwani100@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Jyoti Punjwani
Learning, unless connected to real life, is of not much use. A teacher shares how she instils critical thinking, adaptability and decision-making skills in her students while connecting and applying her subject to real life.

item.title: From village vistas to victory: my odyssey in English mastery
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Ramesh Sandha

In the sun-scorched outskirts of South Telangana, my childhood bloomed like a fragile flower in a storm. Born to a marginalized family, I was cradled by humble parents whose unlettered hearts brimmed with courage. Telugu, my mother tongue, flowed like a gentle river, but English loomed like a towering mountain, distant and daunting.

At 11, English entered my life—a stranger with strange symbols. My teacher, stern as a winter wind, wielded words like weapons, his cane cracking at my missteps. I stumbled through reading and writing, a clumsy colt in a race I couldn’t win. Classmates’ laughter, sharp as thorns, dubbed me the “mocking   bird” of mistakes. Yet, my love for English flickered like a stubborn spark.

A beacon broke the fog when my uncle, a scholar scarred by his own lack of English, whispered, “Wrestle with English, no matter the thorns. Never surrender.” His words became my mantra.

Teacher-centric struggles: My early education was a teacher-centric tyranny. Lessons demanded rote recitation—poems stanza by stanza, essays line by line. I craved immersion, like with Telugu, but found barren drills. I felt like a seed buried too deep, yearning for sunlight but given stones. By 10th grade, I scored a heart-breaking 34 in English, one mark shy of passing. I despised the system’s shackles, but my passion burned bright.

Moments of mockery stung. Holding an English newspaper, I was jeered as “the Englishman”, my speech mocked like a jester’s jest. Yet, my uncle’s words—“Never surrender”—sang like a lighthouse in the storm. I vowed to transform trials into triumphs.

Student-centric awakening: I embraced a student-centric mindset, seeking the encouragement I lacked. Marriage brought a radiant milestone: my wife, from an English-medium background, her eloquence a polished mirror. She sculpted my pronunciation into crisp cadences and guided my grammar, turning tangled sentences into streams of clarity. I devoured radio broadcasts, movies, and stories, embracing corrections as pearls. My efforts bore fruit: a dazzling 90% in English, second topper at the mandal level. I pursued further studies—a B.Ed. and graduation from Osmania University with honors, an M.Ed. from Kakatiya University with distinction, and an MA in English from Andhra University. Today, I serve as a government school English teacher, my classroom a canvas of creativity.

Teacher-centric vs. student-centric legacy: I’ve traded teacher-centric rigidity for a student-centric sanctuary. My classroom is a garden, not a factory—students bloom through vibrant methods inspired by my struggles, my wife’s guidance, and a rich tapestry of professional growth. Completing the TESOL Core Certificate Program (TCCP) in 2023—a 140-hour blended-learning journey with a teaching practicum—I mastered foundational ESL pedagogy and adolescent learner strategies, honing methods like role-play and debates that let shy voices soar like kites. Additional TESOL certifications in Foundations, Teaching Adolescent Learners, and Integrating Technology in ESL Classrooms empowered digital diaries—blogs or podcasts—that connect English to students’ worlds. Storytelling circles, where students weave tales, mirror the immersion I craved, now enhanced with digital tools. Peer mentoring fosters growth, pairing strong speakers with novices. The TESOL Training for Trainers, a 60-hour course, equipped me to mentor peers, a country boy now touching global horizons. As a devoted reader of Teacher Plus, I’ve drawn inspiration from its pages, learning innovative strategies and stories that ignite my teaching style with creativity. Its insights fuel my methods, ensuring every learner finds inspiration, as I once yearned. Teaching is my choice, rooted in compassion, amplified by global standards.

My proudest moment sparkled like a rare dawn when NCERT called me for training, their accolades a golden crown for my perseverance. Friends gathered to cheer, their voices a warm chorus, yet my misty eyes, glistening like dew on a mournful morning, searched for my uncle—the whisperer of my dreams. His absence struck a silent note in my symphony of success—he had passed away, leaving me aching to share this triumph, a longing that lingers like an unfinished verse. Yet his mantra, “never surrender,” echoes in my heart, a guiding star that fuels my mission.

This odyssey transcends mastering English—it’s a testament to passion’s power, carving paths through stone like a relentless river. As an English teacher, armed with TESOL’s wisdom and innovative methods, I dismantle barriers, ensuring my students soar where I once stumbled. My story is a soaring song of hope, sung for every dreamer who dares to defy the odds and never surrender. To echo this melody globally, I share my journey on platforms like Medium and LinkedIn, inviting educators and dreamers worldwide to join this transformative chorus, inspired by a country boy’s leap to a global stage.

Ramesh Sandha is PGT English, TSWRS Boys, Kandukur, Rangareddy, Telangana, India. He can be reached at rameshpaulsandha@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ramesh Sandha
It takes courage, conviction, and persistence to achieve something. Read the author's inspiring story of how he mastered a skill and is now t raining students in that same skill.

item.title: Carols, Chaos and Secret Santa: A Teacher’s Christmas Chronicles
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

December in school is a season of organized chaos disguised as festive cheer. As a teacher, I’ve had the joy—and occasional mischief—of celebrating Christmas with my students and colleagues, observing everything from glitter explosions to carol rehearsals that could double as comedy sketches.

The preparations begin weeks in advance. Classrooms transform into miniature winter wonderlands—or slightly lopsided Christmas corners. Tinsel migrates to unexpected places, baubles wobble dangerously on trees, and glitter somehow finds its way onto everyone’s uniform. Watching students decorate is like supervising a circus act: enthusiasm, chaos, and the occasional “Oh no, the star fell again!” moment.

One of the most delightful parts is the Carol Assembly. Weeks of rehearsals build to a grand performance where students sing with gusto, sometimes improvising lyrics that are equal parts hilarious and heartfelt. My favourite line this year: “Jingle all the way, with joy and cheer today!” There’s always that magical moment when a class forgets the words entirely and everyone hums along, proving enthusiasm beats perfect pitch any day.

Then comes the Secret Santa, a festival highlight. Permission from the school authorities ensures it’s inclusive and safe, but the guessing game begins immediately. Who gave the chocolate bar? Who gifted the quirky pencil? Teachers join in too, often turning it into a friendly competition for the funniest or most imaginative gift. Watching students’ wide-eyed speculation and colleagues’ playful rivalry is heartwarming—and endlessly amusing.

Food is another important part of the celebration. Festive treats—cookies, fruitcake, and the occasional homemade surprise—appear in classrooms, prompting delighted squeals and the inevitable “just one more slice” plea. Sharing these small treats reinforces the true spirit of Christmas: generosity, community, and a little indulgence.

What I love most about Christmas in school is the togetherness. It is in the laughter over slightly crooked decorations, the pride in a perfectly sung carol, and the warmth of Secret Santa exchanges. The festive spirit is colourful, occasionally chaotic, and always heartfelt.

Celebrating Christmas at school reminds me that the season is not just about decorations or presents—it’s about creating memories, sharing laughter, and enjoying those small moments that make school life magical. So, this December, join in the carols, embrace the classroom chaos, and celebrate with students and colleagues alike. After all, in school, Christmas isn’t just celebrated—it is lived, laughed over, and jingle all the way!

Priyanka Soman is an educator and writer with a keen interest in integrating the arts, sciences, and storytelling to create meaningful learning experiences. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Priyanka Soman
It is Christmas time! It is time for cheer and celebrations. What do Christmas celebrations in school mean for you?

item.title: Reimagining geography pedagogy
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sheraz Ahmad Lone and Rouf Ahmad Khan

Geography is often perceived as a subject confined to maps, definitions, and theoretical concepts. But, the subject offers immense scope for moving beyond rote memorization and textbook-centric pedagogy. This article explores how geography education can be transformed through fieldwork, technology integration, and community engagement. It provides practical strategies for teachers to make geography more interactive, relevant, and engaging for students in line with NEP 2020 and NCF guidelines, which advocate a shift towards competency based and experiential learning.

Why move beyond textbooks?

Traditional geography teaching often relies on memorization, making the subject dull and disconnected from real life.  National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 encourages a shift towards critical thinking, hands-on learning, and the use of technology to deepen understanding. Geography directly impacts our daily lives and students need more than just textbooks to understand the subject. By incorporating fieldwork, local case studies, and digital tools, learning becomes engaging, relevant, and meaningful, helping students connect classroom knowledge to the world around them.

Strategies for teaching geography beyond textbooks

Fieldwork and local exploration

Real geography isn't just about reading books, it's about exploring the world around us. When students walk through fields, cross streams, or climb hills, they see geography come to life. Books can explain erosion, but watching soil wash away in the rain makes it real. Field trips change geography from boring facts to exciting discoveries. Walking through a forest helps students feel how weather changes under the trees better than any picture. Seeing plastic waste pile up along a stream/river makes pollution more than just numbers. Even digging in the school garden can teach about soil and rocks.

Bringing geography to life with technology

Technology can turn abstract geography lessons into interactive, hands-on learning experiences that students can see and explore.

  • Google Earth and digital maps: Students can zoom into their local area, compare past and present satellite images, and mark important features like rivers, forests, settlements, and mountains peaks. Platforms like NCERT’s BHUVAN allow students to study detailed maps of India, helping them visualize landscapes and human settlements.
  • Weather apps: Simple weather apps can show real-time temperature, rainfall, and wind patterns. Drones (or even smartphone cameras) can capture aerial views of fields, water bodies, or urban areas, helping students analyze land use and environmental changes.
  • Virtual Reality (VR): If field trips are difficult, VR can take students on digital journeys to mountains, lakes, or deserts. They can "walk" through different terrains, observe erosion patterns, or explore ecosystems--all from the classroom.

These tools make geography more engaging by letting students explore, measure, and interact with real-world data. Instead of just reading about landforms or climate, they can see them, track changes, and even predict future trends-turning them into young geographers.

Community-based learning

Geography isn't just about land and water; it's about how people live with and change their environment. Students can learn directly from their own communities:

  • Talk to farmers: Visit local fields and ask elders how farming has changed. What crops grew 20 years ago? How have rains and temperatures affected harvests? This shows real climate change impacts.
  • Study water systems: Explore how villages traditionally managed water through wells, ponds, or canals. Compare these with today's pipes and pumps. Which methods work better in droughts?
  • Meet disaster volunteers: Work with local groups that prepare for floods or earthquakes. Learn warning systems, safe building tips, and how communities protect themselves.

By learning from farmers, elders, and helpers in their own area, students connect textbook lessons to real people and places.

Project-based learning (PBL)

Students can dive deep into geography by working on simple projects that solve local problems. They could create detailed maps of their local area, marking important features like water sources, farms, and roads while studying how the landscape is changing. Other engaging projects might include investigating pollution in nearby streams, rivers, or fields, interviewing farmers about crop patterns, or analyzing population changes in their community. For practical life skills, students could develop disaster preparedness plans - identifying safe zones during floods or earthquakes and creating emergency kits for their homes or schools. These long-term projects make geography come alive by connecting classroom lessons to students' daily lives, while developing research, teamwork, and problem-solving skills that go beyond textbooks.

Hands-on learning: using clay models to teach geography

Clay models provide a creative and interactive way to learn key geography concepts. Students can shape a spherical clay globe to mark latitudes (like the equator and tropics) and longitudes (such as the prime meridian), helping them visualize Earth’s grid system for locating places. By moulding raised areas for mountains and carving river channels, they can observe how rivers originate in highlands, flow downstream due to gravity, and form drainage patterns. This tactile method enhances spatial understanding, making abstract ideas like coordinates and river systems, the structure of Earth more concrete and engaging for learners.

Challenges and solutions

While these activity-based teaching methods significantly enhance learning, schools often encounter practical hurdles in their implementation. Field trips require careful planning and resources, while access to digital tools like computers and mapping software may be limited. However, schools can overcome these constraints by collaborating with nearby educational institutions, cluster head schools, and local organizations that may share resources and expertise.

The examination-oriented education system sometimes makes it challenging to incorporate hands-on learning. Yet, innovative teachers can seamlessly integrate practical projects with prescribed syllabus topics. Many educators also need support in adopting new technological teaching aids. Fortunately, systematic teacher training programs conducted by SCERTs and DIETs are equipping teachers with necessary digital skills through regular capacity building initiatives.

With these support systems and creative approaches, schools can successfully transition to more engaging, experiential geography education that benefits both students and teachers alike. The key lies in leveraging available resources while adapting methods to local contexts and needs.

Conclusion

Geography education should move beyond textbook learning to include hands-on exploration, digital tools, and real-world connections. By following the NEP 2020 and NCF guidelines, teachers can create engaging lessons where students actively investigate their surroundings. Field trips, local projects, and technology, help students see geography in action. When learners study their own communities/surroundings, they develop practical knowledge and problem-solving skills. This experiential approach not only makes geography more interesting but also prepares students to understand environmental challenges and think of sustainable solutions. By connecting classroom lessons to the real world, we help young geographers become informed citizens who can positively impact their local environments.

Recommendations for teachers

  1. Start small--incorporate one fieldwork/project activity as assessment.
  2. Use free digital tools like Google Earth, NCERT’s BHUVAN.
  3. Collaborate with local experts (farmers, environmentalists).
  4. Document and publish student projects to inspire others.

By reimagining geography education, teachers can nurture a generation of environmentally conscious and analytically skilled citizens.

References:

NEP,2020, https://www.education.gov.in/sites/upload_files/mhrd/files/NEP_Final_English_0.pdf

NCF FS https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/NCF_for_Foundational_Stage_20_October_2022.pdf

Rouf Ahmad Khan is a teacher in the Department of School of Education, JK.

Sheraz Ahmad Lone is is a teacher educator at District Institute of Education and Traings, Anantnag, JK and is also pursuing a doctorate in geography from the Department of Geography, University of Kashmir. The author is also a certified expert in the development of social science textbooks and other curricular materials from NCERT, New Delhi. He holds NET-JRF and PGDDM. He has published about 20 research papers in national and international journals [ springer, Elsevier] and also presented more than 20 papers at a number of national and international conferences. He can be reached at sherazdietang@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sheraz Ahmad Lone and Rouf Ahmad Khan
What does teaching geography mean to you? Map-reading, administering textbook lessons, and providing a fact file? If we want our students to engage with geography, we have to become more creative with our pedagogy. Try introducing field trips, project-based learning, clay modelling, and technology to bring geography alive in your classrooms.

item.title: AI: the new teacher on the block?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Smitha Prasad

Will AI replace teachers? That’s a question I often hear these days.

Well, by the end of this article, you will find me saying no, it won’t. But AI will transform the role of educators in profound ways.

First, let us answer some questions.

Would you rather talk to a customer service executive or a chatbot to solve your problem?

Would you prefer having a cute little puppy or an AI-enabled robot that behaves like one?

Of course, I would want to speak to a customer service executive and play with a real pup even if it soils my carpets and chews on my shoes!

But why do we still choose the real ones even though the virtual ones work efficiently and give accurate information? The key is emotion. That’s what we look for.

Technology can detect emotions, it can sense moods through sound, facial expressions, choice of words, etc. It might say it understands our feelings, but it’s only programmed to do so…it does not actually feel. It might not be able to differentiate between a sarcastic “wow!” and a “wow” used in appreciation. But as humans, we are smart and sensitive enough to note the difference.

AI can definitely help educators. It can provide extensive information and has the potential to teach and solve problems efficiently. There are numerous AI tools that can help teachers plan lessons, design presentations, analyze student performance and data, and even create reports — while you and I can relax! (Most of these tools come with a fee though!)

Students, too, can use AI tools as their assistants to write creative content, essays, and even complete their homework. As teachers, we often can’t tell whether a piece of work was done by a student or by AI. That leads to an important discussion - is AI really helping students learn or just helping them finish tasks?

While AI can help us in many ways, things get complicated when there is decision-making involved. Technology has no idea what emotions feel like. The behaviour of AI-enabled devices is based on algorithms, past data, and experiences of human beings.

Let’s take a classic example often used to explore decision-making – the train track dilemma!

There are two train tracks, one currently in use and the other unused. A few children are playing on the unused track, but many are playing on the used track as well, even as a train is approaching. What should the driver do? Continue on the used track, risking the lives of many children, or switch to the unused one, where only a few are playing? This is a heart wrenching situation, isn’t it?

If the train were AI-driven, what would it decide? While a human driver might hesitate, torn by emotion, AI would act instantly based on its programming. Its decision would depend on what the programmers thought, what data was fed into it, and countless other factors. What seems emotionally impossible for a human might be a simple calculation for AI.

Now imagine a teacher facing decisions like these!

A teacher’s job is not just to give information to the students, it’s a very complex social-emotional-intellectual relationship, which is a balanced equation comprising teaching, mentoring, monitoring, parenting, disciplining, and empathizing with students. And the equation is different with every child in the class. 

A teacher's day is full of simple and complex situations, which need a lot of analyzing, problem-solving, and decision-making. Sometimes it’s as small as just listening to a child, smiling at them, or noticing their new haircut.

There are times when similar answers are written by two students, I write the remarks as “good job!” for one student and “can do better!” for the other - not because I am biased! But because I know each student’s journey. “Good job!” is for the one who’s improved, and “Can do better!” is for the one I know can push further. When we try to programme a teacher or turn one into a robot with artificial intelligence, there are countless factors to consider — and natural intelligence truly matters!

I keep saying this – yes, definitely AI is the new protagonist, but we are the directors! Our roles as educators may change, and the way we operate may evolve — but machines can never replace humans!

Smitha Prasad is an electronics and communications engineer, working for the Brigade Foundation, Bengaluru. She is a curriculum advisor for the Department of Computer Science. She can be reached at smithagprasad@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Smitha Prasad
The core of a teacher-student relationship is emotions. Yes, exchange of knowledge, sharing of information, and guiding students is a teacher’s primary responsibility. But these are things that a well-developed AI tool can do as well. In this day and age what separates a human teacher from AI is her ability to connect with her students emotionally.

item.title: A real-world spin on the five laws of library science
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Satakshi Sharma

School libraries are not just shelves of books; they are spaces that can spark children’s curiosity and support learning. Dr. S. R. Ranganathan, regarded as the father of library science in India, introduced the Five Laws of Library Science to promote user-centred and effective libraries. As an English teacher, my classroom experiences and interactions with students inspired this exploration of his ideas. This article examines Ranganathan’s Five Laws and connects them with essential soft skills such as listening, empathy, and flexibility to strengthen the role of school libraries.

How we tackled it

We went with a qualitative approach, focusing on real stories. We interviewed school librarians, watched libraries in action, and got feedback from students and teachers through surveys. Then we looked for patterns to link soft skills to each of the Five Laws.

Breaking it down

  • Law 1: Books are for use: This one’s about making books easy to reach. It takes initiative and responsibility to set up a space that children want to hang out in. Picture a library where the shelves are arranged so anyone can find what they need fast.
  • Law 2: Every reader his/her book: This is about finding the right book for each child, no matter their interests or reading level. Empathy and listening are key. For example, a librarian might notice a shy reader loves comics and suggest the perfect graphic novel.
  • Law 3: Every book its reader: Some books sit lonely on the shelf, so creativity and communication help them get noticed. Think colourful book displays or fun reading challenges that make children curious about new titles.
  • Law 4: Save the reader’s time: Nobody likes wasting time hunting for a book. Clear signs, smart systems, and quick help make things smooth. Time management and organization are important here, like setting up an online catalogue.
  • Law 5: The library is a growing organism: Libraries have to keep up with new tech and changes. Staying adaptable and eager to learn keeps things fresh, like adding e-books or librarians picking up new skills at workshops.

Change can be tough. Some librarians might not vibe with new ideas, or there is no training for soft skills. Budgets are often tight and small projects can be hard to scale up.Run workshops to help librarians and staff level up their soft skills. Start programs where students can be library leaders. Weave library skills and life skills into classes. Get school leaders to support these ideas with clear policies.

Soft skills add heart to school libraries, making them places that children love to explore. Libraries are always changing, and mixing these skills with the Five Laws can create a special space. It is about blending the structure of library science with the warmth of human connection.

Satakshi Sharma works at Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan Vidyashram Pratapnagar, Jaipur. She can be reached at bvb.sa@jprvidyashramprtp.ac.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Satakshi Sharma
Libraries should be inviting and engaging for children to want to come and explore them. Here’s how you can combine the Five Laws of library science with soft skills to make your school library the most happening hang out among the students.

item.title: Home away from home
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Divya Mishra

It was the first day of the new academic year. A child in a torn uniform had beaten up a few children. He was hiding under the table, looking for a safe place, where he would be heard. He was seeking someone who would listen to him, someone with whom he could share his feelings. Instead of asking him to come out, I went under the table to talk to him and said, "You know what, this is the safest place I have ever seen." I will never forget his expression; he was relieved as if he were in the safest place with someone who was thinking just like him.

That day marked the beginning of a relationship where he never hesitated to talk to me about anything he felt, liked, or hated. 

He struggled with his academics and had a hard time with his friends because of his behaviour, but still, he became a child I trusted. I told him that he was special to me, that he would have friends, and that he could reach high. 

He became so close to me that he started enjoying school. His parents and I worked hard to provide him with a safe environment, both at home and at school. He began to improve and behave well. Now, he is one of the top performers, excelling academically, and is a good classical singer too. 

Building a safe space for children in classrooms not only enriches the classroom, but also changes their lives altogether. A safe environment not only helps facilitators finish their lessons on time and achieve maximum academic success, but it also empowers children to express their thoughts confidently for the rest of their lives. 

Is creating a safe environment a difficult job?  

The answer to this question is certainly yes, as you need to allow children the freedom to express what they want to speak about, whether it's having lunch in the first period or taking a nap before the lesson starts. Now, returning to the same question, is it really difficult? The answer lies in how much control you can let go of, so that you can think about the more important aspects. 

At Aarambh Preschool, we believe that every child should have the confidence to communicate with adults and speak fearlessly. And we are able to make this happen because we provide a free, non-judgmental space to share, whether it involves questioning management decisions or breaking stereotypes. In this journey of more than a decade, I have learned that the amount of free and safe environment you create for teachers automatically reflects in the foundation of the organization, which is the children. 

We, as leaders, should take responsibility for teachers, as they are the ones who will handle children with care, just as we handle teachers with love and care.

Is social-emotional learning important?

Social-emotional learning plays a key role in building such an environment, often involving storytelling. One thing I believe in is the power of listening. The ability to listen is very low these days, as no one seems to have the time.

Children have so much to express, especially when they are struggling to understand their emotions. As teachers, it is important to provide them with the space to share what they want, assured that they will be heard.

Empathy is one important aspect that stands out the most for me. It's hard to imagine what children experience at every age; if we empathize, understand, and help them, we can make a difference.

Sometimes we tend to focus on the things that we can always see, but we forget to see those things that are not visible. We work on the behaviour of the child, but we don't try to understand the why behind the behaviour. It's time to focus on such intangible things. This can only happen when you are emotionally connected to each child. The bond that a teacher forms with their students can push the child's limits.

Imagine a child discovering a safe place to learn and share, creating a home away from home for them.

Divya Mishra is Academic Head, Aarambh Preschool, Raipur, Chhattisgarh. She can be reached at divya@aarambhschool.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Divya Mishra
Children, young or old, need a space where they feel safe, safe enough to express themselves, ask questions, and share without fear. In such spaces, children will blossom into complete individuals. As adults we have to put in the effort to make our schools and classrooms such places for our children.

item.title: Making science come alive
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla

As teachers we often face the challenge of making abstract scientific concepts clear and memorable for our students. Topics like eclipses can, especially, be tricky. Diagrams in textbooks explain the mechanics, but for many children, they remain flat and confusing. I wanted to find a way to make the lesson more engaging and ensure that students not only understood but also remembered it. That’s when I turned to drama and storytelling.

The story idea

I framed the lesson as a short story in which three powerful celestial friends — the Sun, the Moon, and the Earth — play a game of hide-and-seek in the sky. To bring this alive, I invited three students to enact the roles while I narrated the story. Immediately, the classroom atmosphere changed. What could have been a routine explanation became a small theatre performance with the rest of the class watching intently.

Scene 1: The solar eclipse

Moon stepped in front of Sun and spread out their hands dramatically. Earth looked up and exclaimed, “My skies are darkening in the middle of the day!” Sun looked at Moon and said, “You little prankster, let me shine again soon.”

Laughter rippled through the classroom, but at the same time, the scientific point became clear: a solar eclipse occurs when the moon moves between the sun and the earth, blocking sunlight for a brief period. Only those standing in the moon’s shadow can see it.

Scene 2: The lunar eclipse

Next, it was Earth’s turn to move between Sun and Moon. As Earth slowly blocked Sun’s rays, Moon acted out in confusion — “Hey! I’m turning red. What’s happening?”

The narrator explained how the earth’s atmosphere bends some sunlight, giving the moon a reddish glow during a lunar eclipse. This performance helped the students grasp why lunar eclipses look different from solar eclipses and why more people can see them.

How students responded

The energy in the room was remarkable. Even students who are usually shy or reserved wanted to participate, either in the enactment or in the lively discussion that followed. Many asked thoughtful questions: Why doesn’t an eclipse happen every month? Can we see both eclipses from anywhere on earth? The enactment had sparked curiosity, which led to a deeper exploration of the topic.

When I asked the class to explain the difference between solar and lunar eclipses, almost everyone answered confidently. A week later, I was delighted to see that they still recalled the concepts and even recreated the mini-drama on their own. The playful performance had made the learning stick.

Why drama works in science?

Drama turns a lesson from passive listening into active participation. Students are not just hearing about a concept — they are seeing it, feeling it, and sometimes even embodying it. The combination of movement, dialogue, and imagination helps anchor new knowledge in their memory.

Science is full of processes that are invisible to the naked eye: atomic movement, planetary motion, and energy transfer. Drama allows us to make these processes visible in the classroom. More importantly, it makes the learning joyful. When students laugh, act, and engage emotionally, they remember far more than when they only copy diagrams into their notebooks.

Conclusion

My eclipse lesson reinforced my belief that drama integration can transform science teaching. With just a simple story and three student volunteers, I was able to create a lesson that was lively, interactive, and effective. The children not only enjoyed themselves but also gained a clear and lasting understanding of solar and lunar eclipses.
