Drama in the classroom does not require elaborate props or scripts. Even small enactments can make big differences in how students connect with science. As teachers, if we can bring creativity and storytelling into our practice, we can make learning both meaningful and memorable.

Try this in your classroom

  • Use props: A torch (sun), a large ball (earth), and a smaller ball (moon) can help students visualize how shadows form during eclipses.
  • Role rotation: After one group enacts the drama, invite another group to take over — this ensures every child gets to participate.
  • Extend the story: Ask students to script their own dialogues for Sun, Moon, and Earth, or even create a sequel — “What happens if other planets join the game?”
  • Link to art: Encourage students to draw posters of solar and lunar eclipses after the activity, reinforcing their learning through visual expression.

Sravanthi Datla teaches science and is the Primary Coordinator at Westberry School, Bhimavaram, Andhra Pradesh. She can be reached at sravanthi.datla@westberry.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla
Science is considered a practical subject, hands-on, as many concepts can be taught through experiments. But not all science can be taught through experiments. Here is where storytelling comes in handy. Create and act out stories to learn scientific processes that are invisible to the naked eye.

item.title: Teaching festivals using puppets
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
K Sasi Sowjanya

Festivals are an essential part of Indian culture, reflecting our diversity, values, and traditions. But how do we move beyond rote textbook learning to help children experience their essence? I explored this challenge through an engaging puppet story in my grade 3 classroom.

Once upon a time, there was a mother puppet named Festo - a warm, wise woman who loved celebrating festivals. She had three children:

  • Natio, who loved his country and stood tall with the Indian flag.
  • Harvi, who loved harvests and helped with preparations during Pongal and Onam.
  • Religi, who found joy in worship, lights, and spiritual songs during Diwali, Eid, and Christmas.

Each of her children represented a type of Indian festival:

  • Natio -> National Festivals (Independence Day, Republic Day)
  • Harvi -> Harvest Festivals (Pongal, Bihu, Onam, Lohri)
  • Religi -> Religious Festivals (Diwali, Eid, Christmas, Gurpurab)

As the puppets spoke and acted in class, students easily identified festival types, their values, and how they connect with real life.

Classroom integration activities

  • Costume day: Children dressed as Natio, Harvi, and Religi and shared stories about their favourite festivals.
  • Puppet-making workshop: Students created their own festival puppets with paper and cloth.
  • Festival tree chart: We made a chart showing how different festivals fit under the three categories.
  • Reflection journal: Students wrote or drew their favourite puppet and what they learned.

Impact

This story-based approach helped children understand why we celebrate festivals, not just what we celebrate. It also encouraged collaboration, creativity, and emotional connection.

References

  1. NCERT Grade 3 Social Studies Curriculum
  • Getting Ahead in Social Studies textbook (used in class)
  • National Portal of India (https://www.india.gov.in)

K. Sasi Sowjanya is a primary social studies educator in Westberry High School, Bhimavaram with a passion for creative and experiential learning. She integrates storytelling, drama, and puppetry into her lessons to make learning meaningful and joyful for young learners.She can be reached at sasi.sowjanya@westberry.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
K Sasi Sowjanya
Festivals are a big part of Indian culture and it is important that our children know about them. Yes, there are many books in the market to teach children about our different festivals, but is there another way to involve children in a lesson on festivals?

item.title: Why literacy learning centres make for smart learning
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Supriya Prakash

Literacy learning centers are more than just classroom add-ons — they are smart, intentional tools that bring structure, equity, and measurable impact into the literacy classroom. Done well, centers create space for autonomy, differentiation, teacher-led focus, and authentic evidence of learning.

If the science of reading has taught us anything, it is this: teaching with heart and effort is not enough. What truly matters is whether instruction is working for children and      whether learning can be measured in authentic, organic ways that inform and refine whole-group instruction. Literacy learning c     enters give us exactly that — a feedback loop that pushes instruction toward equity, challenge, and productive struggle.

At the same time, they align beautifully with the developmental needs of young learners, offering short, sharp bursts of focus — 10 to 15 minutes at a time — which is exactly the right cognitive window for early childhood classrooms.

Autonomy building agency in learners

A literacy center is not a worksheet station. It is a space where children can actively try out strategies they have learned, experiment with tools, and engage in productive struggle without constant teacher intervention.

Instead of stepping in too quickly, the teacher deliberately withholds scaffolding, leaving behind quick reference charts or task cards that nudge children to think independently. Materials are multisensory and concrete, suited to the developmental stage of young learners.

Within this space, children learn not only skills but also the art of task completion — collaborating, dabbling, and learning from peers, all without the teacher hovering over their shoulder. This nurtures confidence, independence, and authentic engagement.

Differentiation: meeting learners where they are

Equity in instruction is no longer optional — it is the expectation. Effective teachers know that not all children need the same task, the same way, at the same time. Literacy centers provide an elegant solution.

By designing centers at multiple levels of complexity, teachers ensure that students have access to tasks at the sound, word, and sentence level. Every child has an entry point: some revisit and practice foundational skills, while others extend themselves into more complex applications.

This form of differentiation is natural and non-stigmatizing. Instead of being singled out for one-on-one intervention (which can feel like “me versus the teacher”), children find themselves in a majority setting where everyone is working at their level. Teachers can      observe      or join small groups briefly, offering just-in-time nudges.

The result is a classroom where every child blooms at their own pace.

Teacher-led work

While autonomy and independence are central to learning centers, certain skills demand direct, careful guidance. Letter formation, phoneme awareness, and sound–symbol correspondence are examples of foundational tasks that, if learned incorrectly, are very hard to unlearn.

Teacher-led centers provide this precision. In these small-group sessions, children receive the teacher’s full attention, but in a way that is time-bound and purposeful. For the teacher, these moments offer more than skill correction — they provide a window      into the child’s thinking process, fueling authentic assessments and meaningful parent-teacher conversations.

Here, scaffolds can be deeply personalized, making learning accessible and tailored, while still keeping the environment dynamic and engaging.

Evidence of learning: data that feeds instruction

Perhaps the most powerful feature of literacy centers is the evidence they generate. Centers are designed with outcomes in mind. Teachers set clear expectations for what children should demonstrate, and the tasks provide authentic data points that directly inform next steps.

Because learning centers typically remain in place for one to two weeks, children have repeated opportunities to practice and consolidate their skills. This “rinse and repeat” approach helps students move from exposure to mastery — building confidence along the way.

The formative data gathered through observation, checklists, and student work is both quick and authentic. It reflects not just whether teaching happened, but whether learning stuck.

Over time, these cycles of evidence allow teachers to adapt instruction “just in time” instead of “just in case,” ensuring teaching is always responsive and relevant.

The bottom line

Phonics, word work, and literacy centers are not just classroom activities — they are powerful engines of equitable, evidence-based teaching. They:

  • Give children autonomy and agency.

  • Support natural differentiation without stigma.

  • Provide targeted, teacher-led intervention where it matters most.

  • Generate authentic data that sharpens whole-group instruction.

When designed thoughtfully, literacy learning centers embody the balance between structure and flexibility, between independence and guidance. They create classrooms where every child can thrive — not someday, but today.


Supriya Prakash is a Literacy Coach with 18 years of experience as a teacher, HOD, and mentor. Certified in IB and TCWRP literacy coaching, she blends academic expertise with a passion. She can be reached at Supriya.prakash2@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Surprya Prakash
Literacy learning centers are presented as structured, evidence-based tools that support autonomy, differentiation, and targeted teacher instruction. Supriya Prakash explains how these centers can help to generate useful data that can align with the developmental needs of early learners.

item.title: Nurturing hearts, building humane values
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Vikram Thakur

At The Aarambh School, education is not confined to textbooks or classrooms — it is about shaping compassionate hearts and empathetic minds. Every year, the school celebrates Daan Utsav – the Festival of Giving, to help children experience the joy of sharing and imbibe values such as gratitude, empathy, and respect.

As part of this initiative, our students visit various NGOs and institutions — old age homes, orphanages, schools for children with special needs, homes for the visually impaired, government hospitals and animal shelters. Each visit is a journey of awareness and transformation, helping children see life beyond their own world.

One such visit was to an ashram for mentally challenged children. A student shared, “A child there kept looking at my watch with such excitement! I never even think about my watch, but it meant so much to him.” That simple observation taught him the true meaning of gratitude — to value what we often take for granted. Another student reflected, “They were so joyful! They didn’t envy anyone and were happy all the time.” Such reflections show how children begin to understand that happiness lies in contentment, not in possessions.

The experience led to an even more touching act. One student, deeply moved, said, “Can we all come together and buy watches for them?” The group immediately contributed, and within two days, they were ready with the gifts. When they returned to the ashram and handed out the gifts, the joy on everyone’s faces was indescribable — a moment that captured the pure essence of giving.

Another heartwarming experience happened during a visit to an old age home. The children interacted, danced, and shared stories with the elderly residents. The happiness on the seniors’ faces was unmistakable — a reflection of how deeply they missed their own grandchildren. After returning, a group of students approached me and said, “Sir, can you please help us go back to meet them? Or maybe we can get the phone numbers of their children? We want to tell them that no one should ever leave their parents” Hearing such words from 10- and 11-year-olds brought tears to my eyes. In that moment, I knew — we are truly nurturing a better society.

Our students also visited an animal shelter, where they saw injured and paralyzed dogs — some victims of accidents, others harmed intentionally. Witnessing their suffering moved the children deeply; many of them cried. Since then, their attitude toward animals has changed — they no longer throw stones or act carelessly, but instead show sensitivity and kindness toward every living being.

Another memorable visit was to the railway station, where students served food to those in need. One man came up to me and said, “I came to this city today to appear for a competitive exam. I somehow missed my last train and didn’t have money to stay back or buy food. I was praying to God — and then I saw the children serving meals. I felt that God truly listens!” I shared this incident with my students  and his words made them realize how even the smallest acts of kindness can make a world of difference.

During Daan Utsav, our celebrations also include acts of appreciation for our support staff. This year, children brought food from home and served it to them, expressing their gratitude for the hard work and care they receive every day. The smiles, laughter, and sense of togetherness made it a truly special moment for everyone.

Through such experiences, Daan Utsav at The Aarambh School has become more than a celebration — it is a journey of emotional growth. It teaches our children that giving is not just about material things; it is about sharing joy, spreading kindness, and building connections that touch the heart.

These moments remind us, as educators, that education is truly complete when it awakens the spirit of humanity.

As it is said, “The deeds of giving are the very foundations of the world.”

And at the end of the day, it’s not about what you have or even what you have accomplished — it’s about who you have lifted up, who you have made better, and what you have given back.

Vikram Thakur is the co-founder of The Aarambh School. He believes that education is the most powerful tool to bring positive change in society. Through The Aarambh School, his vision is to nurture a generation that is not only knowledgeable but also empathetic, compassionate, and responsible — individuals who contribute meaningfully to building a better world. He can be reached at vikram@aarambhschool.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vikran Thakur
At The Aarambh School, children experience, imbibe and practice values of empathy and gratitude. Visits to ashrams, old age homes, hospitals, and animal shelters, reshape how they see happiness, kindness, and responsibility.

item.title: Building psychological strength of adolescent Indians
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Pradeep Waychal

Indian adolescents are navigating a complex landscape of academic pressure, rapid digitalization, high family expectations, and career uncertainties. One in seven Indian adolescents experiences mental health challenges, resulting in increased instances of depression, anxiety and stress      (Source– A 2024 National Mental health Survey). Developing psychological strength can help manage stressful thoughts, regulate emotions, and improve productivity.

Psychological strength is a complex and multi-dimensional concept.      We have defined it to consist of Gratitude, Resilience, Achievement-orientation, Positivity, Emotional intelligence, and a Sophophilic (knowledge-oriented) mindset – collectively called GRAPES Strengthening GRAPES      can ensure adolescents not only survive, but also thrive, leading to a healthy and progressive society and nation.

Gratitude

Gratitude is a thankful appreciation for what an individual receives, both tangible and intangible. It shifts focus from scarcity to abundance, enhancing contentment and motivation. Gratitude also helps people connect to something larger than themselves – to other people, nature, or a higher power, and initiates a virtuous cycle. Individuals practicing daily gratitude journaling reported a 23% reduction in stress levels and a 17% improvement in sleep quality (Source- 2024- study by Indian Council of Medical Research).

Resilience

Resilience is the ability to withstand adversity and bounce back from failures. This is especially critical in India, where limited opportunities often lead to a higher rate of failures. Resilient individuals also experience stress and emotional upheaval but they manage and control such situations with focus, perseverance and grit. The resilience is not innate and can be systematically built through self-reflection and learning from setbacks, along with mentoring.

Achievement orientation

The progress of humanity is primed by the passion to achieve something significant in life - to set lofty goals and accomplish them. Achievement orientation aligns efforts with purpose. It goes beyond mere academic grades. It includes taking calculated risks and persisting through challenges. It helps channelize energy towards one’s goals. It requires embracing deep learning and seeing setbacks as opportunities for growth, rather than defining one’s worth.   

Positivity

Positivity is the ability to identify opportunities within challenges, while acknowledging reality. It is the practice of focusing on the good in any situation and looking at setbacks as temporary. Positivity enables adolescents to reframe challenges, support creative problem-solving and have a positive impact on their physical and mental health. Individuals who engaged in positive self-talk and reframed negative experiences showed a 28% improvement in their perceived quality of life (Happiness Survey India (2024)).

Emotional intelligence

Emotional Intelligence (EI) involves recognizing, understanding, and managing emotions in oneself and others in positive ways to relieve stress, communicate effectively, empathize with others, and defuse conflict. It helps in handling interpersonal challenges and nurturing meaningful relationships, reducing isolation and increasing support systems during adversity. A 2023 NASSCOM Talent Report revealed that leaders with high EI outperformed their peers by 20% in managing team conflicts and building trust.

Sophophilic mindset

A Sophophilic mindset -- or love for learning -- goes beyond information gathering into developing wisdom. It expands perspectives and increases adaptability. It fosters curiosity, humility, and open-mindedness. An All India Survey on Higher Education report (2024) noted that students engaged in voluntary learning projects outside their curriculum, demonstrated 25% higher problem-solving skills and adaptability - the critical 21st century skills.

How can parents and others develop a GRAPES mindset

The simplest way is they themselves live as per the above six dimensions. They can present and discuss case-studies of individuals who have displayed these strengths. Developing GRAPES needs equal importance and attention as academics. Promote frank conversations with adolescents so that they can freely discuss their feelings and challenges without fear of judgment. Inculcate the habit of ‘journaling’ in adolescents.

For gratitude, adolescents can regularly share things for which they are grateful with their near and dear ones. For resilience, adolescents must attempt solving problems by themselves to the extent possible or with support form parents. They must learn to accept failures and parents must assure them that failures don’t define their worth. For achievement orientation, adolescents can be guided to set their missions and micro-goals aligned with it. They should be praised for their methods, not just the results. Positivity can be inculcated by reframing challenges through self-evaluation and looking at the silver lining. Emotional intelligence can be developed via reflective exercises and active debates. A Sophophilic mindset can be cultivated by developing lifelong learning habits. This includes promoting reading and structured learning of new topics through real-life projects.

Conclusion    

In a rapidly evolving India, psychological strength will be a bridge between potential and realized achievements. By nurturing a GRAPES mindset, we can      build a society and country that is both materially and spiritually successful.

Dr. Pradeep Waychal is a Managing Trustee at Guruji Education Foundation. He can be reached at pradeep.waychal@gurujifoundation.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pradeep Waychal
With rising academic pressure, digital exposure, and career uncertainty, Indian adolescents face growing mental health challenges. This article introduces the GRAPES framework as a structured approach to building psychological strength.

item.title: What changes can be made in education?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati

Education is an essential pillar of society. A country whose education is competent can reach new heights of development. In today's modern era, when technology and scientific discoveries are constantly advancing, there is a need to improve our educational sector according to the times. For the new generation, there should be such an education that is not limited to just book knowledge, but teaches them how to face the complex situations of life.

Practice-based education    

 Beyond books, children should be taught things that are useful in life. Such as — banking, time management, health, and first aid. Currently, Bagless Day is being celebrated in schools. As part of this celebration, we can give children  practical knowledge in these areas. We can make children visit banks, hospitals, and visit various businesses and industries. We can assign children various activities so that they learn time management. Role play can also be taught on how to help others in times of disaster.

Technology-based education    

 Education can be made more interesting and easy through digital platforms, virtual classrooms and AI-based tools. Students should be encouraged to do projects and multimedia presentations. For this, smart classrooms have been built in almost all schools in Gujarat. Online  quizzes can help them self-evaluate and see what mistakes they have made. Children can also be taught coding through applications like Scratch and Picto Block. Through this, children gain knowledge of technology as their decision-making power and intelligence develop.      

Interdisciplinary approach    

  When multiple subjects are taught in a single activity or project, it is called an interdisciplinary approach where we can combine lie skills and technology with subjects like mathematics, science, history and language. For example, data analysis in mathematics, digital presentation in history, and so on. With this approach, children can learn better by combining different subjects and gain direct experience from life. Here’s an example:    

Example : Water saving campaign

Subjects : Science + Mathematics + Language + Art

Activity : Teaching ways to save water.

  1. Make them calculate how much water is used in their daily routine.
  • Write a formula on the topic of ‘Save Water’.
  • Make a poster.

This activity connects the following subjects:    

Science: by highlighting the importance of water

Mathematics: through water consumption accounting

Language: formula/text

Art: Poster

Balance of knowledge and skills    

 Professional skills should also be developed in the student, wherein IT, digital marketing, graphic design, multimedia, handicrafts etc. should be taught. The child should have both the ability to know and do. Knowledge means getting information, understanding things, knowing (or being able to find) the answers to questions, and learning rules and principles. For example, knowledge implies knowing that water boils at a temperature of 100 degrees Celsius. However, skill means working with what you know, and using the experience of study in life. If there is only knowledge and no skills, a person would know a lot but have no ability to apply their knowledge. If there is only skill and no knowledge, a person can do the work, but would not have the ability to understand the reason for it. Therefore, the balance of both is very important in the all-round development of a      child. “It is necessary to know and also to show!” Skill without knowledge is incomplete and knowledge without skill is like the pages of an empty book. Together, both create true all-round development.

Life values ​​and moral education    

 Moral education is also important for instilling humanism, patriotism, understanding of right and wrong and inspiration for living in students. The good principles, ways of doing things and humane values ​​in our life, which we adopt in life, are called life values. For example, speaking the truth, being kind, cooperating, working hard, being honest, etc. All these are the main foundations of knowledge, behaviour and personality. The approach to teaching children good qualities, culture and morality is moral education. The education that makes them understand the difference between right and wrong, makes them good citizens, teaches them discipline, humanity, and responsibility is called moral education.      

Innovative training for teachers    

 It is also necessary to train teachers in new technology and teaching methods, so that they can prepare students as per the needs and requirements of the rapidly changing modern world.    

Conclusion         

In summary, it can be said that education is not only a tool for imparting knowledge, but also for overall personal and social development. If timely changes are not brought, the education system will become traditional and the future generation will be left behind. Therefore, the new era requires an education system that develops technology, life values, practical skills and the ability to face the problems of life.

Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati is a teacher at the Primary School in Gujarat’s Kheda district. He can be reached at prajapatisandy1989@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sandipkumar Natvarbhai Prajapati
Moving beyond textbook learning, this piece highlights how interdisciplinary learning can make education more relevant and engaging for today’s students.

item.title: Silent struggles: the mental health crisis among private school teachers
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Rajvir Singh

In a time when education is evolving rapidly, the mental health of those imparting knowledge—our teachers—is increasingly under threat. Once considered a stable and fulfilling profession, teaching, especially in private schools, has become a high-pressure occupation riddled with stress, exhaustion, and emotional burnout.

Today’s private school teacher wears many hats: instructor, event organizer, data handler, and sometimes even counsellor. The workload has ballooned far beyond classroom instruction. Teachers not only conduct back-to-back lessons but also carry home heaps of notebooks for correction, prepare lesson plans, manage student behaviour, and plan extra-curricular events. The concept of “free time” is a distant dream.

"Teaching used to be a noble and relaxed profession. Now, I feel like I’m constantly racing against the clock," says a private school teacher who wished to remain anonymous.

Adding to this stress is the increasing interference from parents. Teachers often face unrealistic expectations and frequent complaints—ranging from student performance to teaching styles. The pressure to please parents can lead to manipulation of grades or ignoring disciplinary issues, further impacting the teacher’s sense of integrity and control in the classroom.

"Sometimes, I spend more time justifying my methods to parents than actually planning lessons for my students," one educator shared.

What’s more, many teachers feel stifled by a lack of autonomy. Despite their training and experience, school managements often impose rigid policies, dictate curriculum content, and demand frequent documentation. The trust and respect once naturally extended to educators have, in many cases, been replaced with surveillance and scepticism.

Technology, too, has contributed to the problem. While it has improved accessibility and connectivity, it has also blurred the boundaries between personal and professional life. Teachers are now expected to be available after school hours on messaging apps and online platforms, responding to queries and assigning or evaluating homework digitally.

All these factors contribute to a steep decline in teachers' mental well-being. Issues such as chronic fatigue, anxiety, depression, and burnout are alarmingly common but remain largely unaddressed due to lack of institutional support or mental health resources within schools.

What’s the way forward?

Experts suggest that the solution lies in structural reforms. Redistributing workloads, setting boundaries on parent-teacher interactions, conducting mental health awareness programmes, and offering professional counselling are steps in the right direction. School administrations must also create channels for open communication and feedback, where teachers can voice concerns without fear of judgment or reprisal.

If we truly value education, we must start valuing      educators. Supporting their mental health is not just an act of compassion—it’s an investment in the future of our children.

Rajvir Singh is a mathematics teachers at Paramount Public School, Lehra District, Sangrur, Punjab. He can be reached at Me.rajvir61@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Rajvir Singh
From back-to-back lessons and endless corrections to after-hours digital availability, private school teachers face an ever-expanding workload. This piece explores how these pressures are affecting teachers’ mental health and what structural reforms may be needed.
